name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ قَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولُنَا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ كَثِيرًا مِّمَّا كُنتُمْ تُخْفُونَ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَيَعْفُوا۟ عَن كَثِيرٍ ۚ قَدْ جَآءَكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ نُورٌ وَكِتَـٰبٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٥)
```

```english
O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you much of what you used to hide of the Book, and excusing many [an offense of yours]. Certainly, there has come to you a light from Allah, and a manifest Book. (15)
```

```arabic
يَهْدِى بِهِ ٱللَّهُ مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ رِضْوَٰنَهُۥ سُبُلَ ٱلسَّلَـٰمِ وَيُخْرِجُهُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ بِإِذْنِهِۦ وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (١٦)
```

```english
With it Allah guides those who follow [the course of] His pleasure to the ways of peace, and brings them out from darkness into light by His will, and guides them to a straight path. (16)
```

```arabic
لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ۚ قُلْ فَمَن يَمْلِكُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا إِنْ أَرَادَ أَن يُهْلِكَ ٱلْمَسِيحَ ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَأُمَّهُۥ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا ۗ وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ۚ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٧)
```

```english
They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary.’ Say, ‘Who can avail anything against Allah should He wish to destroy the Messiah, son of Mary, and his mother, and everyone upon the earth?’ To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and whatever is between them. He creates whatever He wishes, and Allah has power over all things. (17)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ نَحْنُ أَبْنَـٰٓؤُا۟ ٱللَّهِ وَأَحِبَّـٰٓؤُهُۥ ۚ قُلْ فَلِمَ يُعَذِّبُكُم بِذُنُوبِكُم ۖ بَلْ أَنتُم بَشَرٌ مِّمَّنْ خَلَقَ ۚ يَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ۖ وَإِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٨)
```

```english
The Jews and the Christians say, ‘We are Allah’s children and His beloved ones.’ Say, ‘Then why does He punish you for your sins?’ No, you are humans from among His creatures. He forgives whomever He wishes, and punishes whomever He wishes, and to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and whatever is between them, and toward Him is the return. (18)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ قَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولُنَا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ عَلَىٰ فَتْرَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلرُّسُلِ أَن تَقُولُوا۟ مَا جَآءَنَا مِنۢ بَشِيرٍ وَلَا نَذِيرٍ ۖ فَقَدْ جَآءَكُم بَشِيرٌ وَنَذِيرٌ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٩)
```

```english
O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying [the Divine teachings] for you after a gap in [the appearance of] the apostles, lest you should say, ‘There did not come to us any bearer of good news nor any warner.’ Certainly, there has come to you a bearer of good news and a warner. And Allah has power over all things. (19)
```

## (Commentary)

When the Exalted mentioned His taking of the covenant (الميثاق) from the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) to support His messengers, to honor them, and to preserve what He gave them of the Book, and then mentioned their breaking of His covenant which He had bound them with, He called them to believe in His Messenger whom He sent and His Book which He revealed. He did this by way of introducing both to them, establishing clear proof (البينة) for the truthfulness of the message and the veracity of the Book, and completing the proof (إتمام الحجة) against them in that regard. As for the introduction, it is contained in His saying: **"O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you much..."** (5:15), and His saying: **"O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you after a gap..."** (5:19).

As for the establishment of clear proof (البينة), it is found in His saying: **"clarifying for you much of what you used to hide..."** (5:15). Indeed, that is an excellent witness to the truthfulness of the message coming from an unlettered man (أمي) who informs of that which there is no way to know except for the elite among their scholars. Likewise is His saying: **"With it Allah guides those who follow [the course of] His pleasure..."** (5:16), for the true objectives whose veracity is beyond doubt are an excellent witness to the truthfulness of the message and the veracity of the Book.

As for the completion of the proof (إتمام الحجة), it is what is contained in His saying: **"lest you should say, ‘There did not come to us any bearer of good news nor any warner.’ So there has certainly come to you a bearer of good news and a warner. And Allah has power over all things"** (5:19). Within these verses, Allah the Exalted has also refuted the claim of some: **"They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary’"** (5:17), and the claim of the Jews and Christians: **"We are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones"** (5:18).

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ قَدْ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولُنا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ كَثِيراً مِمَّا كُنْتُمْ تُخْفُونَ مِنَ الْكِتابِ وَيَعْفُوا عَنْ كَثِيرٍ** — **"O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you much of what you used to hide of the Book, and bypassing many [others]."** As for his clarification of much that they used to hide of the Book, it is like his clarification of the verses of prophecy and its glad tidings, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **"Those who follow the Apostle, the unlettered prophet, whom they find written with them in the Torah and the Evangel..."** (7:157), and His saying, the Exalted: **"they recognize him just as they recognize their sons"** (2:146), and His saying: **"Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves... That is their description in the Torah and their description in the Evangel"** (48:29). It is also like the clarification by the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of the ruling regarding the man [the adulterer] which they had concealed and over which they had stubbornly resisted the truth, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted, which will follow: **"O Apostle! Do not let those who outpace each other in faithlessness grieve you..."** (5:41). This ruling—meaning the ruling of stoning (الرجم)—is present even now in the twenty-second chapter of the Book of Deuteronomy in the Torah currently circulating among them.

As for his bypassing (عفوه) many [others], it refers to his leaving aside much of what they used to hide of the Book. This is evidenced by the discrepancies found in the two Books [the Torah and the Gospel], such as the Torah containing matters regarding monotheism (التوحيد) and prophecy (النبوة) that cannot correctly be attributed to Him, the Exalted, such as anthropomorphism (التجسم) and incarnation in a place (الحلول في المكان) and the like; and what the intellect (العقل) deems impossible to attribute to the noble prophets, such as various types of disbelief, immorality, and lapses; and the Torah’s total lack of mention of the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and the life hereafter* — whereas no religion can stand except upon the foundation of the Return; and the inclusion in the Gospels they possess, especially the Gospel of John, of paganistic beliefs.

His saying, the Exalted: **قَدْ جاءَكُمْ مِنَ اللهِ نُورٌ وَكِتابٌ مُبِينٌ** — **"Certainly there has come to you from Allah a light and a manifest Book."** The apparent meaning of His saying: **"there has come to you from Allah"** is that this "comer" subsists through Him, the Exalted, in a manner of subsistence like that of a clarification (البيان) or speech (الكلام) subsisting in the clarifier or the speaker. This supports the view that what is meant by the "light" (النور) is the Quran. Based on this, His saying: **"and a manifest Book"** (وَكِتابٌ مُبِينٌ) would be coordinated to it as an explanatory coordination (عطف تفسير), meaning both "the light" and "the manifest Book" refer to the Quran. Allah the Exalted has named the Quran "light" in several places in His speech, such as His saying, the Exalted: **"and follow the light that has been sent down with him"** (7:157), and His saying: **"So believe in Allah and His Apostle and the light We have sent down"** (64:8), and His saying: **"and We have sent down to you a manifest light"** (4:174).

It is also possible that what is meant by "the light" is the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), as might be suggested by the opening of the verse. Allah the Exalted has counted him as a light in His saying: **"and as a radiant lamp"** (33:46).

His saying, the Exalted: **يَهْدِي بِهِ اللهُ مَنِ اتَّبَعَ رِضْوانَهُ سُبُلَ السَّلامِ** — **"With it Allah guides those who follow [the course of] His pleasure to the ways of peace."** The preposition "with" (بـ) in His saying: **"With it"** (بِهِ) denotes the instrument (الآلة), and the pronoun refers back to "the Book" or to "the light," whether the latter refers to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) or the Quran. The ultimate result is the same, for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is one of the apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرية) in the stage of guidance (الهداية), as is the Quran, while the reality of guidance (حقيقة الهداية) subsists through Him [Allah]. He, the Exalted, said: **"You cannot guide whomever you wish, but Allah guides whomever He wishes"** (28:56), and He said: **"Accordingly We have enlightened you with a spirit from Our command. You did not know what the Book is, nor what is faith; but We made it a light by which We guide whomever We wish of Our servants, and indeed you guide to a straight path, the path of Allah, to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth. Behold, all matters return to Allah"** (42:52-53). These verses, as you see, attribute guidance to the Quran and to the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while simultaneously returning it to Allah, the Glorified. Thus, He is the Guide in reality (حقيقة), and others are apparent causes (سبب ظاهري) subjugated to bring the matter of guidance to life.

The Exalted qualified His saying: **"With it Allah guides"** with His saying: **"those who follow [the course of] His pleasure."** This amounts to conditioning the actualization of divine guidance upon the following of His pleasure. Thus, what is meant by guidance (الهداية) here is the "conveyance to the objective" (الإيصال إلى المطلوب), which is that Allah leads him into one of the ways of peace (سبل السلام), or all of the ways, or most of them, one after another.

The Exalted left "peace" (السلام) absolute; thus, it is safety and deliverance from every wretchedness that disrupts the happiness of life in this world or the hereafter. This accords with how the Quran describes submission (الإسلام) to Allah, faith (الإيمان), and godwariness (التقوى) as prosperity (الفلاح), triumph (الفوز), and security (الأمن), and the like. It has already been mentioned in the discussion on His saying, the Exalted: **"Guide us on the straight path"** (1:6) in the first volume of this book, that Allah, the Glorified, possesses many ways (سبل) according to the differing states of His traveling servants, all of which unite in a single path attributed to Him, the Exalted, which He calls in His speech "the straight path" (الصراط المستقيم). He, the Exalted, said: **"As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them in Our ways, and Allah is indeed with the virtuous"** (29:69), and He, the Exalted, said: **"This is indeed My straight path, so follow it, and do not follow [other] ways, for they will separate you from His way"** (6:153). This indicates that He has many ways, but they all unite in leading to His dignity, without separating their travelers or distinguishing the travelers of one way from the travelers of another, as is the case with ways other than His path.

The meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: Allah, the Glorified, guides and leads, by means of His Book or by means of His Prophet, whoever follows His pleasure into ways whose nature is such that whoever travels them is safe from the wretchedness of the life of this world and the hereafter, and from everything that embitters a happy life.

Thus, the matter of guidance to peace and happiness revolves around following the pleasure of Allah. He, the Exalted, has said: **"He does not approve of faithlessness for His servants"** (39:7), and He said: **"Allah is not pleased with the transgressing lot"** (9:96). Ultimately, this depends on avoiding the path of wrongdoing (الظلم) and refraining from joining the ranks of the wrongdoers (الظالمين), for Allah, the Glorified, has denied them His guidance and made them despair of attaining this divine dignity by His saying: **"and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot"** (62:5). Thus, this verse—meaning His saying: **"With it Allah guides those who follow [the course of] His pleasure to the ways of peace"**—runs in a sense like His saying: **"Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing—for such there shall be security, and they are the guided ones"** (6:82).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَيُخْرِجُهُمْ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ بِإِذْنِهِ** — **"and He brings them out from darkness into light by His will."** The pluralization of "darkness" (الظلمات) and the singularization of "light" (النور) is an indication that the path of truth (الحق) has no discrepancy or division within it, even if it multiplies according to stations and positions, unlike the path of falsehood (الباطل).

When the "bringing out from darkness into light" is attributed to someone other than Him, the Exalted, such as a prophet or a book, the meaning of His "permission" (إذنه) therein is His authorization and pleasure, as He, the Exalted, said: **"A Book We have sent down to you that you may bring mankind out from darkness into light by the command of their Lord"** (14:1). He qualified his bringing them out from darkness into light with the permission of their Lord to exclude independence in causality (الاستقلال في السببية), for the true cause (السبب الحقيقي) of that is Allah, the Glorified. He also said: **"Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs: ‘Bring your people out from darkness into light’"** (14:5). He did not qualify it with "permission" here because the command itself contains that meaning.

When this is attributed to Allah, the Exalted, the meaning of bringing them out "by His permission" is bringing them out "by His knowledge" (بعلمه). The word "permission" (الإذن) has come with the meaning of knowledge; it is said: **أذن به** meaning "he knew of it." From this category is His saying, the Exalted: **"An announcement from Allah and His Apostle"** (9:3), and **"Say, ‘I have proclaimed [it] to you all alike’"** (21:109), and His saying: **"And proclaim the Hajj to the people"** (22:27), among other verses.

As for His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ** — **"and guides them to a straight path,"** the word "guidance" (الهداية) has been repeated because His saying: **"and He brings them out"** intervened between His saying: **"With it Allah guides"** and this sentence. Furthermore, because the straight path (الصراط المستقيم)—as explained previously in Surah al-Fatihah—is a path that oversees (مهيمن) all other paths, the guidance to it is also a guidance that oversees all other types of guidance related to particular ways (السبل الجزئية).

The indefiniteness (التنكير) of His saying: **"a straight path"** (صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ) does not contradict the fact that what is meant is the unique straight path which Allah, the Exalted, attributes in His speech to Himself—except in Surah al-Fatihah—because the context indicates that. Rather, the indefiniteness is to magnify its status and emphasize its importance.

His saying, the Exalted: **لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ** — **"They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary.’"** These are one of the three groups whose views were previously cited in Surah Al 'Imran, namely those who believe in the union (اتحاد) of Allah, the Glorified, with the Messiah, such that he is God and man simultaneously. It is possible to apply this sentence—meaning their saying: **"Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary"**—to the belief in sonship and to the belief in "the third of three" as well, although the apparent meaning of the sentence is the attainment of identity (العينية) through union (الاتحاد).

His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ فَمَنْ يَمْلِكُ مِنَ اللهِ شَيْئاً إِنْ أَرادَ أَنْ يُهْلِكَ الْمَسِيحَ ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَأُمَّهُ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً** — **"Say, ‘Who can avail anything against Allah should He wish to destroy the Messiah, son of Mary, and his mother, and everyone upon the earth?’"** This is a proof (برهان) to invalidate their claim by way of its internal contradiction. For when they posited that the Messiah, despite being God, was a human being as they described him as "the son of Mary," they allowed for him what is allowed for any human being assumed to be among the inhabitants of this earth. All of them, like all other parts of the heavens and the earth and what is between them, are owned (مملوكون) by Allah, the Exalted, and are subjugated under His ownership and authority. Thus, He, the Exalted, has the right to dispose of them as He wishes, and to judge for them or against them however He wills. He has the power to destroy the Messiah just as He has the power to destroy his mother and everyone on earth equally, without any distinction for the Messiah over others. And how can destruction be possible for Allah, the Glorified?! Thus, their positing that the Messiah is a human being invalidates their positing that he is Allah, the Glorified, due to the contradiction.

So His saying: **"Who can avail anything against Allah"** is an allusion (كناية) to the absolute negation of any preventer. To "own" (يملك) something against Allah means to have authority (السلطنة) over Him, the Exalted, in some matter pertaining to Him, the consequence of which would be the cessation of His authority over that thing. This would mean that some cause among causes acts independently in its effect on something such that it prevents His effect or overcomes Him in it. But there is no ownership (ملك) except for Allah alone, who has no partner, except for what He has granted others to own in a way that does not invalidate His own ownership and authority.

In His saying: **"should He wish to destroy the Messiah, son of Mary, and his mother, and everyone upon the earth,"** He only qualified the Messiah with the phrase **"son of Mary"** to demonstrate that he is a complete human being subject to Lordly influence like all other humans. For that same reason, He coordinated **"his mother"** to him, as she is undoubtedly of the same genus (مسانخة) as him, and coordinated **"everyone upon the earth"** to him, because the ruling applies to all equally.

From here it becomes clear that in this qualification and coordination there is a hint toward the "proof of contingency" (برهان الإمكان). Its summary is that the Messiah is similar to other human individuals, such as his mother and everyone else on earth; thus, what is possible for them is possible for him, because the ruling for similar things (الأمثال) regarding what is possible and what is impossible is one. Since it is possible for others to fall under the ruling of destruction, it is possible for him as well, and there is no preventer to prevent it. If he were Allah, the Glorified, this would not be possible for him.

His saying: **"To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them"** serves as the rationale (تعليل) for the preceding sentence. The explicit mention of **"whatever is between them,"** despite the Quran often expressing the world of creation as "the heavens and the earth" only, is to make the speech closer to being explicit and safer from the entry of delusions and doubts. Thus, no deluded person can imagine that He mentioned the heavens and the earth but did not mention what is between them, while the subject of the discussion is among what is between them.

The advancement of the predicate (تقديم الخبر)—meaning His saying: **"To Allah"** (وَلِلَّهِ)—is to denote exclusivity (الحصر), and with that the explanation is completed. The meaning is: How can any preventer prevent His will, the Exalted, to destroy the Messiah and others, and the occurrence of what He willed of that, when absolute ownership (الملك) and authority in the heavens and the earth and what is between them belong to Allah the Exalted, and no one else has any ownership? Thus, there is no preventer to stop the execution of His judgment and the passage of His command.

His saying: **"He creates whatever He wishes, and Allah has power over all things"** serves as the rationale for the sentence preceding it, namely His saying: **"To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them."** For "sovereignty" (المُلك)—with a *damma* on the *mim*—which is a type of authority and ownership over the authority of people and what they own, is only constituted by the encompassment of power (القدرة) and the efficacy of will (المشيئة). Allah, the Glorified, possesses that in all the heavens and the earth and what is between them. He has power over all things and He creates whatever things He wishes. Thus, He has absolute sovereignty in the heavens and the earth and what is between them. His creating whatever He wishes and His power over all things is the proof for His sovereignty, just as His sovereignty is the proof that He has the right to wish the destruction of all and then execute His will if He so wished. It is also the proof that no one among them shares in His divinity (ألوهيته).

As for the proof for the efficacy of His will and the encompassment of His power, it is that He is Allah, exalted is His Name. Perhaps for this reason, the majestic name "Allah" was repeated several times in the verse. Thus, the assumption of divinity in anything leads to the conclusion that He has no partner in His divinity.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَقالَتِ الْيَهُودُ وَالنَّصارى نَحْنُ أَبْناءُ اللهِ وَأَحِبَّاؤُهُ** — **"The Jews and the Christians say, ‘We are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones.’"** There is no doubt that they were not claiming actual sonship (النبوة الحقيقية) as most Christians claim for the Messiah (peace be upon him); neither the Jews nor the Christians were claiming that in reality. Rather, they were applying it to themselves as an honorific title (إطلاقا تشريفيا) by way of a type of metaphor (تجوز). This usage has appeared frequently in their sacred books, as in the case of Adam, Jacob, David, Ephraim, and Jesus, and it was also applied to the righteous among the believers.

In any case, what was meant by "sons" is that they are to Allah, the Glorified, in the position of sons to a father. They are like the sons of a king in relation to him, set apart from the subjects and distinguished by the characteristic of proximity (القرب), which necessitates that they not be treated like the common subjects. It is as if they are exempted from the execution of laws and rulings applied among the people, because their connection to the throne of the king is not compatible with their being punished as others are punished, nor with their being made to stand where the rest of the subjects stand. They are not to be held in contempt as others are. All of this is because of the bond of love and honor that follows the bond of lineage.

Thus, what is meant by this "sonship" is special distinction and proximity. The coordination of His saying: **"and His beloved ones"** to His saying: **"the sons of Allah"** is like an explanatory coordination, though not strictly so. Their purpose in claiming this distinction and belovedness was to establish its necessary consequence (لازمه), which is that there is no way to punish or penalize them; they will only ever attain grace and honor, because His punishing them would contradict the special merit He granted them and the honor He bestowed upon them.

The evidence for this is what appeared in the refutation against them in His saying, the Exalted: **"He forgives whomever He wishes and punishes whomever He wishes."** For if they did not mean by their saying: **"We are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones"** that there is no way to punish them even if they did not respond to the true call, there would be no reason to mention this sentence: **"He forgives..."** as a refutation against them, nor would His saying: **"Rather you are humans from among His creatures"** have a fitting and appropriate place. So the meaning of their saying: **"We are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones"** is: We are the elite of Allah and His beloveds; He has no way to punish us regardless of what we do or what we leave, because the negation of the way [to punishment] and the occurrence of total security from every disliked and feared thing is the necessary consequence of the meaning of distinction and love.

His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ فَلِمَ يُعَذِّبُكُمْ بِذُنُوبِكُمْ** — **"Say, ‘Why then does He punish you for your sins?’"** He commanded His Prophet to argue against them and refute their claim with proof. These are two proofs: the first is a refutation (النقض) based on the punishment actually occurring to them, and the second is a counter-argument (المعارضة) with a proof that yields the opposite of their claim.

The summary of the first proof, contained in His saying: **"Why then does He punish you for your sins?"** is that if your claim were true—that you are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones, secure from divine punishment and that there is no way for it to reach you—then you would be secure from every punishment, whether in the hereafter or in this world. So what is this punishment occurring to you and continuing among you because of your sins? As for the Jews, they have never ceased committing sins, such as their killing of their prophets and the righteous among their people, their transgression in breaking the divine covenants taken from them, their distorting of words from their places, their concealing of the signs of Allah and disbelieving in them, and every form of tyranny and aggression. They have tasted the evil consequence of their affair as an exemplary punishment (نكالا) upon them, from the transformation (مسخ) of some of them to the striking of abasement and poverty upon others, and the domination of wrongdoers over them who kill them, violate their honor, and destroy their lands. They have no life except the life of one who is wasting away (الحرض)—neither alive such that he is hoped for, nor dead such that he is forgotten.

As for the Christians, the corruption of the acts of disobedience and sins occurring among their nations is no less than what was among the Jews, nor are the types of punishment that descended upon them before the mission [of the Prophet], during its time, and after it until today. History preserves all of that or most of it for them, and the Quran recounts much of it, as in Surah al-Baqarah, Al 'Imran, al-Nisa, al-Ma'idah, al-A'raf, and others.

It is not for these people to say: "These calamities, afflictions, and trials descending upon us are merely of the category of 'affliction for the sake of loyalty' (البلاء للولاء), and there is no evidence that they stem from divine wrath that drags along exemplary punishment and evil consequences. Similar things descended upon the righteous servants of Allah among the prophets and messengers, such as Abraham, Ishmael, Jacob, Joseph, Zechariah, John, and others, and similar things descended upon you, O community of Muslims, as in the battles of Uhud, Mu'tah, and others. So why is it that when these misfortunes befall us, they are counted as divine punishments, but when they befall you, they become blessings and honors?"

This is because there is no doubt for anyone that these physical misfortunes and worldly calamities and afflictions occur to the believers just as they occur to the disbelievers, and they take the righteous and the wicked together—the way of Allah (سنة الله) which has passed among His servants. However, they differ in title (عنوانا) and effect (أثرا) according to the person's position regarding righteousness or wickedness, and the servant's station before his Lord.

There is no doubt that for one in whose soul righteousness has become established and in whose essence human virtue has taken root—like the noble prophets and those who follow them—the worldly calamities and trials descending upon him only result in the actualization of the latent virtues in his soul, from which he and others benefit through their good effects. This type of trial, which contains what the natural disposition (الطبع) dislikes, is nothing but divine training (تربية إلهية), or if you wish, an elevation of rank (ترفيعا للدرجة).

As for one who has not yet settled upon happiness or wretchedness and has not yet taken the necessary path of happiness, when calamities descend upon him and the turns of fortune revolve around him, they result in the determination of his path and the distinction of his position, whether toward disbelief or faith, righteousness or wickedness. This type of affliction and trial should only be called divine examinations and tests (امتحانات وابتلاءات إلهية) that carve a path for the person toward Paradise or toward the Fire.

And as for one who relies in his life only on the desire of the soul (هوى النفس) and is accustomed only to corruption and causing corruption and immersion in the depths of lust and anger, and never ceases choosing vice over virtue and seeking superiority over Allah over submission to the truth—as the Quran recounts of the end of the affair of the wrongdoing nations like the people of Noah, 'Ad, Thamud, the people of Pharaoh, the inhabitants of Midian, and the people of Lot, following their neglect of their duty toward Allah—the misfortunes poured upon them, which destroyed their collective, cannot correctly be counted as anything other than divine punishments (تعذيبات إلهية), exemplary punishments (نكالات), and evil consequences (وبالات) upon them, and nothing else.

Allah the Exalted has gathered these meanings in His saying, and He is the most excellent of speakers: **"We make such days alternate among mankind, so that Allah may ascertain those who have faith, and that He may take witnesses from among you, and Allah does not love the wrongdoers. And so that Allah may purge those who have faith and wipe out the faithless"** (3:140-141).

The history of the Jews from the time of the mission of Moses (peace be upon him) until Allah sent Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—spanning more than two thousand years—and likewise the history of the Christians from the time of the ascension of the Messiah until the appearance of Islam—spanning nearly six centuries according to what is said—is full of the types of sins they committed and crimes they perpetrated, leaving nothing undone, then persisting and acting arrogantly without regret. Thus, the misfortunes befalling their territory deserve nothing but the name of punishment and exemplary penalty.

As for the fact that the Muslims were afflicted with things similar to what these nations were afflicted with, these afflictions, in view of their cosmic nature (طبيعتها الكونية), are nothing but events driven by the hand of divine governance—the way of Allah (سنة الله) which has passed before, and you will never find any change in the way of Allah. In view of the state of the Muslims afflicted by them, as long as they were on the path of truth, they were nothing but divine examinations. But when they deviated from it, they were of the category of exemplary penalty and punishment. No one has a special honor (كرامة) over Allah, nor does any arbitrary person have a right over Him. The Quran did not establish for them any special honor over their Lord, nor did it count them as the sons of Allah and His beloved ones, nor did it pay heed to the names or titles they called themselves by.

He, the Exalted, said, addressing them: **"Do you suppose that you will enter Paradise though Allah has not yet ascertained those of you who have struggled, and He has not ascertained the steadfast? ... Muhammad is but an apostle; [other] apostles have passed before him. If he dies or is slain, will you turn back on your heels? Anyone who turns back on his heels will not harm Allah in the least, and Allah will soon reward the grateful"** (3:142-144). And He, the Exalted, said: **"It is not by your fancies, nor by the fancies of the People of the Book: whoever commits evil shall be requited for it, and he shall not find besides Allah any guardian or helper"** (4:123).

In the verse—meaning His saying: **"Say, ‘Why then does He punish you for your sins?’"**—there is another possibility, which is that what is meant is the punishment of the hereafter, and the present tense verb (**يُعَذِّبُكُمْ**) denotes the future rather than continuity as in the previous view. For the People of the Book admit to punishment in exchange for their sins in general: as for the Jews, the Quran has recounted their saying: **"The Fire shall not touch us except for a number of days"** (2:80). As for the Christians, although they believe in the atonement (الفداء) for the forgiveness of sins, it is in itself an affirmation of the sins and the punishment that befell the Messiah through the crucifixion. Furthermore, the Gospels affirm sins such as adultery and the like, and the Church used to affirm it in practice through the indulgences (صكوك المغفرة) it used to issue. This is so, but the first view is the correct one.

His saying, the Exalted: **بَلْ أَنْتُمْ بَشَرٌ مِمَّنْ خَلَقَ يَغْفِرُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما بَيْنَهُما وَإِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ** — **"Rather you are humans from among His creatures. He forgives whomever He wishes and punishes whomever He wishes, and to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, and toward Him is the return."** This is a second proof presented by way of counter-argument, the summary of which is: Reflection upon your reality leads to the invalidation of your claim that you are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones. For you are humans from among those He created, whether humans or otherwise; you have no distinction over the rest of those Allah created from them. No one in creation, from the heavens and the earth and what is between them, is more than a creature of Allah, who is the Sovereign (المليك) ruling over him and over others as He wishes and however He wishes, and he will return to his Lord, the Sovereign ruling over him and over others. Since it is so, it is for Allah, the Glorified, to forgive whomever He wishes of them and to punish whomever He wishes of them, without any distinction or honor or anything else preventing Him from wishing for something what He wishes of forgiveness or punishment, or any interrupter cutting off His path, or any veil being struck before Him to hide Him from the efficacy of His will and the passage of His judgment.

Thus, His saying: **"Rather you are humans from among His creatures"** is in the position of one of the premises (مقدمات) of the proof. His saying: **"and to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them"** is another premise. His saying: **"and toward Him is the return"** is a third premise. And His saying: **"He forgives whomever He wishes and punishes whomever He wishes"** is in the position of the conclusion (نتيجة) of the explanation, which contradicts their claim that there is no way to punish them.

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ قَدْ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولُنا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ عَلى فَتْرَةٍ مِنَ الرُّسُلِ** — **"O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you after a gap (فترة) between the apostles."** Al-Raghib said: "Languor" (الفتور) is stillness after intensity, softness after hardness, and weakness after strength. He, the Exalted, said: **"O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you after a gap between the apostles,"** meaning a stillness devoid of the coming of a messenger of Allah.

The verse is a second address to the People of the Book, completing the previous address. For the first verse explained to them that Allah sent to them a messenger whom He supported with a manifest Book that guides, by Allah's permission, to every good and happiness. This verse explains that this divine clarification is only to complete the proof against them, lest they say: "There did not come to us any bearer of good news nor any warner."

By this explanation, it is supported that the object of the verb **"clarifying for you"** (يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ) in this verse is the same as that in the previous verse. The estimation is: "clarifying for you much of what you used to hide of the Book," meaning that this religion to which you are called is the very same religion you used to profess, confirming what is with you. What is seen in it of points of discrepancy is only a clarification of what you hid of the religious knowledge (معارف الدين) which the divine Books had clarified. The consequence of this view is that His saying: **"O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you"** is of the category of repeating the exact previous address in order to join some speech separated from the previous address that pertains to it—namely His saying: **"lest you should say..."**—to it. This was permitted because of the long separation between the related parts, which is common in the language. He [the poet] said:

*Bring the hitching post of al-Na'amah close to me,*
*The war of Wa'il has conceived after being barren.*
*Bring the hitching post of al-Na'amah close to me,*
*Indeed, selling the noble for a sandal-strap is costly.*

It is also possible that it is a newly started address (خطاب مستأنف) and the object of the verb **"clarifying for you"** has been omitted to denote generality (العموم)—meaning he clarifies for you everything that needs clarification—or to emphasize its importance—meaning he clarifies for you a great matter that you need clarified. His saying: **"after a gap between the apostles"** is not devoid of an intimation or indication of this need, for the meaning is: he clarifies for you what your need has become urgent for him to clarify, while the time was devoid of messengers to clarify that for you.

His saying: **"lest you should say, ‘There did not come to us any bearer of good news nor any warner’"** is related to His saying: **"has certainly come to you,"** with the estimation: "lest you should say" or "for fear that you say."

His saying: **"And Allah has power over all things"** is as if to repel an objection (دفع الدخل). For the Jews did not see the permissibility of legislating a law after the law of the Torah, due to their belief in the impossibility of abrogation (النسخ) and change in divine decree (البداء). So Allah, the Glorified, refuted their false claim by stating that it contradicts the generality of His power. The discussion on abrogation has already preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **"For any verse that We abrogate..."** (2:106) in the first volume of this book.

## (A Discourse on the Method of Reflection to which the Quran Guides — A Mixed Inquiry)

That which we do not doubt is that human life is an intellectual life (حياة فكرية) that is not completed for him except through the perception (الإدراك) which we call reflection (فكرا). It was a necessary consequence of basing life on reflection that the more correct and complete the reflection, the more upright the life. Thus, an upright life—by whatever tradition (سنة) a person takes, and in whatever path, traveled or untraveled, a person walks—is linked to upright reflection and based upon it. To the extent of its share of it is its share of uprightness.

Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned this in His Mighty Book in different ways and various styles, such as His saying: **"Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people, like one who is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?"** (6:122), and His saying: **"Are those who know equal to those who do not know?"** (39:9), and His saying: **"Allah will raise in rank those of you who have faith and those who have been given knowledge"** (58:11), and His saying: **"So give good news to My servants who listen to the word and follow the best [interpretation] of it. They are the ones whom Allah has guided, and it is they who possess intellect"** (39:17-18), among many other verses that do not need citing. Thus, the matter of the Quran in calling to correct reflection and promoting the way of knowledge (العلم) is beyond doubt.

The Noble Quran, moreover, mentions that what it guides to is a path among intellectual paths. He, the Exalted, said: **"Indeed this Quran guides to that which is most upright"** (17:9), meaning the creed, tradition, or method that is most upright. In any case, it is a vital path (صراط حيوي) whose being most upright depends on the path of reflection within it being most upright. He, the Exalted, said: **"Certainly there has come to you from Allah a light and a manifest Book. With it Allah guides those who follow [the course of] His pleasure to the ways of peace, and He brings them out from darkness into light by His will, and guides them to a straight path"** (5:15-16). The straight path (الصراط المستقيم) is the clear path in which there is no discrepancy or failure—meaning it does not contradict the sought truth, nor do its parts contradict one another.

He did not specify in the Mighty Book this correct and upright reflection to which He invites, except that He referred therein to what people know according to their primordial intellects (عقولهم الفطرية) and the perception embedded in their souls. If you were to trace the Divine Book and then contemplate its verses, you would find what perhaps exceeds three hundred verses containing a call to people to reflect (التفكر), to remember (التذكر), or to use the intellect (التعقل), or teaching the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) the proof (الحجة) to establish a truth or to invalidate a falsehood, such as His saying: **"Say, ‘Who can avail anything against Allah should He wish to destroy the Messiah, son of Mary, and his mother...’"** (5:17). Or it recounts the proof from His prophets and friends, such as Noah, Abraham, Moses, and the rest of the great prophets, and Luqman and the believer from the family of Pharaoh, and others (عليه السلام), such as His saying: **"Their apostles said, ‘Is there any doubt about Allah, the originator of the heavens and the earth?’"** (14:10), and His saying: **"When Luqman said to his son, as he was advising him: ‘O my son! Do not ascribe any partners to Allah. Polytheism is indeed a great injustice’"** (31:13), and His saying: **"A believing man from Pharaoh’s clan who concealed his faith said, ‘Will you kill a man for saying, “My Lord is Allah,” while he has already brought you manifest proofs from your Lord?’"** (40:28), and His saying recounting the sorcerers of Pharaoh: **"They said, ‘We will never prefer you over the manifest proofs that have come to us and [over] Him who originated us. So decide whatever you will decide. You can only decide about the life of this world’"** (20:72), until the end of what they argued.

Allah the Exalted did not command His servants in His Book, not even in a single verse, to believe in Him or in anything that is from Him, or to walk a path blindly while they do not perceive. He even rationalized the laws and rulings He made for them—whose detailed rationales (ملاكاته) the intellect has no way to know—with matters that run the course of arguments, such as His saying: **"Indeed prayer prevents indecencies and wrongs, and the remembrance of Allah is surely greater"** (25:45), and His saying: **"Prescribed for you is fasting as it was prescribed for those before you, so that you may be God-conscious"** (2:183), and His saying in the verse of ablution: **"Allah does not desire to put you to hardship, but He desires to purify you, and to complete His blessing upon you so that you may be grateful"** (5:6), among other verses.

This intellectual perception (الإدراك العقلي)—meaning the path of correct reflection to which the Noble Quran refers and upon whose confirmation it builds what it calls to of truth, good, or benefit, and warns against of falsehood, evil, or harm—is only that which we know by creation and primordial nature (الفطرة). It is that which does not change or alter, and about which no human and another human dispute, nor do two people differ. If a difference or dispute is assumed therein, it is only of the category of quarreling over self-evident truths (البديهيات), ending in the failure of one or both of the quarreling parties to conceptualize the true meaning of the subject of dispute due to a lack of correct mutual understanding.

As for what this path is that we know according to our human primordial nature, if we doubt anything, we do not doubt that there are external, objective, real truths (حقائق خارجية واقعية) independent of our actions, such as the issues of the Origin (المبدأ) and the Return (المعاد), and other mathematical or natural issues. If we wish to attain them with certain attainment (حصولا يقينيا), we rely therein upon primary, self-evident propositions (قضايا أولية بديهة) that are not subject to doubt, and others that follow from them with similar necessity. We arrange them in a specific intellectual order from which we conclude what we seek, such as our saying: "A is B, and every B is C, therefore A is C," and our saying: "If A is B, then C is D; and if C is D, then E is F," which yields: "If A is B, then E is F," and our saying: "If A is B, then C is D; and if C is D, then E is F; but A is not B," which yields: "E is not F."

These forms (الأشكال) we have mentioned and the primary materials (المواد الأولية) we have pointed to are self-evident matters that it is impossible for a human with a sound primordial nature to doubt, except due to an intellectual defect or a confusion in understanding that necessitates the failure to conceptualize these necessary matters by taking another conceptual or propositional concept in place of the self-evident concept or proposition, as is common among those who doubt self-evident truths.

When we review the doubts and objections raised against this aforementioned logical path (الطريق المنطقي), we find that they rely, in concluding their claims and objectives, upon the likes of the laws codified in logic pertaining to form (الهيئة) and matter (المادة), such that if we were to analyze their speech into the initial premises taken therein, it would return to logical materials and forms. If we were to change some of those premises or forms to what logic declares to be non-productive, the speech would become non-productive, and you would see that they would not be satisfied with that. This itself is the clearest witness that these people admit, according to their human primordial nature, the correctness of these logical principles, submitting to them and employing them—"they denied them, though their souls were certain of them."

**1 —** Like the saying of some theologians (المتكلمين): "If logic were a path that leads [to truth], no disagreement would occur among the logicians, but we find them differing in their opinions." He has employed a conditional syllogism (القياس الاستثنائي) without realizing it. This speaker has overlooked that the meaning of logic being an instrument of protection (آلة الاعتصام) is that its use as it ought to be protects the human from error. As for the claim that every user of it uses it correctly, no one makes such a claim. This is just as the sword is an instrument of cutting, but it only cuts through correct use.

**2 —** And the saying of some of them: "These laws were codified and then completed gradually, so how can the establishment of objective truths be based upon them? And how can one who does not know them or does not use them hit upon reality?" This, like the previous one, is a conditional syllogism and of the worst kind of sophistry (المغالطة). The speaker has erred in the meaning of "codification" (التدوين), for its meaning is the detailed uncovering of rules known to the human by primordial nature in summary, not that the meaning of codification is origination.

**3 —** And the saying of some of them: "These principles were only promoted among the people to block the door of the People of the House (أهل البيت) or to divert people from following the Book and the Sunnah, so it is mandatory for Muslims to avoid them." This is speech that can be broken down into categorical and conditional syllogisms. The one arguing with this did not realize that the leveling of a path for a corrupt purpose or its traveling for an unpraiseworthy end does not contradict its uprightness in itself, just as the sword may be used to kill the oppressed, and as religion may be used for other than the pleasure of Allah, the Glorified.

**4 —** And the saying of some of them: "Intellectual traveling (السلوك العقلي) sometimes leads its traveler to what contradicts the explicit Book and Sunnah, as we see from the opinions of many of the philosophizers." This is a categorical syllogism (قياس اقتراني) composed with a sophistry from the perspective that what is forbidden is not the form of the syllogism nor a self-evident matter, but rather a corrupt, foreign matter that entered among the correct materials.

**5 —** And the saying of some of them: "Logic only undertakes to distinguish the productive form from the corrupt form, but as for the materials (المواد), there is no law in them that protects the human from error therein, and one is not safe from falling into error if we refer to other than the People of Infallibility (أهل العصمة). Thus, what is determined is to return to them." In this is a sophistry from the perspective that it was driven to demonstrate the authority of solitary reports (أخبار الآحاد) or the sum of solitary reports and the speculative appearances (الظواهر الظنية) of the Book. It is well known that protection through the infallibility of the People of Infallibility (عليه السلام) only occurs in what we are certain of their speech regarding its issuance and its intended meaning together with true certainty. And where is that obtained in solitary reports which are speculative in issuance and indication? Likewise in everything whose indication is speculative. If the criterion for protection is the certain material (المادة اليقينية), then what is the difference between the certain material taken from their speech and the certain material taken from intellectual premises? The consideration of the form (الهيئة) along with that remains as it is.

And their saying: "We do not obtain certainty in intellectual materials after all these confusions," the response is: Firstly, it is stubborn denial (مكابرة). Secondly, this speech itself is an intellectual premise intended to be used as certain, and the speech contains the form.

**6 —** And the saying of some of them: "Everything that human souls need is stored in the Mighty Book, deposited in the reports of the People of Infallibility (عليه السلام), so what is the need for the leftovers of the disbelievers and atheists?"

The answer to it is that the need for them is the very same need witnessed in this speech itself, for it has been composed as a logical categorical composition, and certain materials have been used in it. But it was sophistically argued, firstly, that those logical principles are part of what is stored and deposited in the Book and Sunnah, and there is no path to them except independent inquiry.

Secondly, the lack of need of the Book and Sunnah and their self-sufficiency from an adjunct joined to them is different from the lack of need of the one who holds fast to them and deals with them. In this is the sophistry. The example of these people is but like the example of a physician researching the human body if he were to claim self-sufficiency from learning the natural, social, and literary sciences because all of them pertain to the human. Or like the example of an ignorant human if he were to refrain from learning the sciences, excusing himself that all sciences are deposited in the human primordial nature.

Thirdly, the Book and Sunnah are the two that call for expansion in the use of correct intellectual paths (which are nothing but self-evident premises or those leaning upon the self-evident). He, the Exalted, said: **"So give good news to My servants who listen to the word and follow the best [interpretation] of it. They are the ones whom Allah has guided, and it is they who possess intellect"** (39:17-18), among other verses and numerous reports. Yes, the Book and Sunnah forbid following what contradicts them with an explicit, definitive contradiction, because the Book and the definitive Sunnah are among the instances of what the explicit intellect has demonstrated to be truth and veracity. It is impossible for the intellect to demonstrate a second time the invalidity of what it demonstrated to be true the first time. The need to distinguish true intellectual premises from false ones and then to hold fast to the true premises is like the need to distinguish the clear (المحكمة) verses and reports from the ambiguous (المتشابهة) ones and then to hold fast to the clear among them, and like the need to distinguish the reports truly issued from the fabricated and interpolated reports, which are numerous reports.

Fourthly, the truth is truth wherever it is and however it is found and from whatever place it is taken. The faith of its carrier or his disbelief, and his godwariness or his wickedness, do not affect it. Turning away from the truth out of hatred for its carrier is nothing but holding fast to the prejudice of ignorance (عصبية الجاهلية) which Allah, the Glorified, has censured, and censured its people in His Mighty Book and by the tongue of His messengers (عليه السلام).

**7 —** And the saying of some of them: "The path of precaution (الاحتياط) in religion, which is encouraged in the Book and Sunnah, is to restrict oneself to the appearances of the Book and Sunnah and to avoid dealing with logical and intellectual principles, for in that is exposure to permanent destruction and wretchedness after which there is never any happiness."

The response is that this explanation itself has employed logical and intellectual principles, for it contains a conditional syllogism in which intellectual premises were taken that are clear to the intellect even if there were no Book or Sunnah.

Furthermore, the clarification is only complete for those whose aptitude is insufficient to comprehend subtle intellectual matters. As for the one possessing the aptitude and capacity for such things, there is no evidence from the Book, the Sunnah, or reason for depriving him of attaining the realities of knowledge (حقائق المعارف), through which alone human dignity and nobility are realized. Indeed, the Book, the Sunnah, and reason have all pointed toward this.

**8 - The saying of some of them—regarding what he mentioned:** "The path of the righteous predecessors (السلف الصالح) was distinct from the path of philosophy (الفلسفة) and gnosis (العرفان). They found sufficiency in the Book and the Sunnah, dispensing with the use of logical and intellectual principles (الأصول المنطقية والعقلية) as the philosophers do, and with the methods of spiritual discipline (طرق الرياضة) as the gnostics do.

Then, when Greek philosophy was translated into Arabic during the era of the Caliphs, the theologians (المتكلمون) among the Muslims—who were followers of the Quran—sought to apply philosophical propositions to Quranic knowledge. Through this, they split into two sects: the Ash'arites (الأشاعرة) and the Mu'tazilites (المعتزلة). Later, others emerged during the time of the Caliphs who called themselves Sufis (الصوفية) and gnostics (العرفاء); they claimed to uncover secrets and possess knowledge of the realities of the Quran, asserting that they had no need to refer to the People of Infallibility and Purity (أهل العصمة والطهارة). By this, the jurists (الفقهاء) and the Shi'a (الشيعة)—who are those who hold fast to their [the Imams'] coattails (عليه‌السلام)—were distinguished from them. Matters remained thus until near the middle of the thirteenth century of the Hijra (approximately one hundred years ago). At that point, these individuals (meaning the philosophers and gnostics) began practicing deception (التدليس), obfuscation (التلبيس), and the interpretation (تأويل) of the objectives of the Quran and Hadith to align with philosophical and gnostic propositions, until the matter became confused for the majority."

He concluded from this that these principles are contrary to the true path (الطريقة الحقة) to which the Book and the Sunnah guide.

Then he raised some objections against logic (المنطق)—similar to those we have mentioned—such as the existence of disagreement among logicians themselves, the occurrence of error despite its use, and the lack of a sufficient amount of self-evident (البديهيات) and certain (اليقينيات) premises in actual matters. He then mentioned many issues from philosophy, counting them all as contradictions to the explicit meaning derived from the Book and the Sunnah.

This is the summary of his discourse, which we have condensed.

Would that I knew which aspect of this discourse, despite its many facets, is capable of reform or repair? Indeed, the disease has overwhelmed the cure.

As for what he mentioned regarding the history of the theologians and their deviation from the Imams (عليه‌السلام), their intent to apply philosophy to the Quran, their subsequent division into the Ash'arite and Mu'tazilite sects, the emergence of the Sufis and their claim that they and their followers were independent of the Book and the Sunnah, the persistence of this state, and the emergence of gnostic philosophy in the thirteenth century—all of this is refuted by definitive history (التاريخ القطعي), and a brief reference to all of this will follow.

Furthermore, there is a gross error in his conflation of theology (الكلام) and philosophy (الفلسفة). Philosophy investigates reality (بحثا حقيقيا) and demonstrates established issues through certain premises (مقدمات يقينية), whereas theology investigates in a manner broader than both the real and the conventional (الاعتباري), and argues for established, posited issues using premises that are broader than the certain and the accepted. Thus, the distance between the two arts is greater than the distance between the heaven and the earth. How then can it be conceived that the people of theology sought, in their theological discourse, to apply philosophy to the Quran? Moreover, the theologians, from the very first of them to emerge until our present day, have remained in conflict with the philosophers and gnostics; their existing books and treatises, and the recorded disputes occurring between them, are the most eloquent witnesses to this.

Perhaps this attribution is taken from the words of some Orientalists who claim that the translation of philosophy into Islam is what created the science of theology (علم الكلام) among Muslims. This claimant was ignorant of the meaning of theology and philosophy, the purpose of the two arts, and the causes necessitating the emergence of theological discourse; he shot without a target.

More amazing than all of that is that he subsequently mentioned the difference between theology and philosophy: that theological inquiry seeks to prove issues of the Origin (المبدأ) and the Return (المعاد) while observing the side of religion, whereas philosophical inquiry seeks that without concerning itself with the matter of religion. He then made this a proof that proceeding by way of logical and intellectual principles is a path distinct from the path of religion and contradictory to the way legislated therein. Thus, he increased the corruption. Every expert knows that whoever mentioned this difference between the two arts intended to point out that the syllogisms employed in theological discussions are dialectical (جدلية), composed of accepted premises: (the widely-known (المشهورات) and the conceded (المسلمات)), because the deduction is for the sake of established issues. Whereas what is employed in philosophical discussions are demonstrative syllogisms (قياسات برهانية) intended to prove what is the truth (الحق), not to prove what has been conceded as established. This is quite different from saying that one of the two paths (the path of theology) is the path of religion and the other is a path distinct from religion that is not to be regarded, even if it be true.

As for his objections against logic, philosophy, and gnosis: what he objected to regarding logic has already been discussed. As for what he mentioned regarding philosophy and gnosis, if what he mentioned is as he described it and understood it, and it then contradicts the explicit truth of religion, then there is no doubt for any doubter that it is false and among the slips and errors of those who investigate philosophy or traverse the path of gnosis. However, the point is that the slips of the practitioners of an art, their failures, and their deviations are not to be laid at the door of the art itself; rather, they are attributed to the deficiency of the investigators in their research.

He ought to have reflected on the disagreements arising among the theologians: the Ash'arites, the Mu'tazilites, and the Imamis. These disagreements split the single Islamic word, turning it initially into seventy-three sects, then further dividing each sect into sub-sects. Perhaps the branches of every root are no less in number than the roots themselves.

Would that I knew: did anything create these disagreements other than following the path of religion? Can any researcher use this as evidence for the falsehood of religion and the corruption of its path? Or does he provide an excuse here that does not apply there, or charge those with a moral vice whose very essence or likeness is not found among these? The science of Islamic jurisprudence (الفقه) is similar to the science of theology in this regard, with its branching into various schools and groups, and the disagreements arising among each group themselves. The same applies to all other sciences and crafts, despite their number and diversity.

As for what he concluded from his entire discourse regarding the invalidity of all practiced methods and the necessity of the path of the Book and the Sunnah—which is the path of religion—he cannot help but see the path of recollection (التذكر), which is attributed to Plato the Greek. This is the idea that if a human being were to strip himself of psychic whims and adorn himself with the ornament of godwariness (التقوى) and spiritual virtues, then return to his own soul regarding a matter, the truth of it would become manifest to him.

This is what they have mentioned, and it was chosen by some of the ancients among the Greeks and others, a group of Muslims, and a faction of Western philosophers. However, each of those who hold this view formulated it in a different way: some formulated it on the basis that human sciences are innate (فطرية) in the sense that they are attained by him and exist with him in actuality (بالفعل) at the beginning of his existence; thus, the meaning of the occurrence of every new piece of knowledge for him necessarily returns to the attainment of recollection.

Others formulated it on the basis that returning to the soul by turning away from material distractions necessitates the uncovering of realities—not in the sense that sciences are with the human being in actuality, but rather they are for him in potentiality (بالقوة), while actuality resides in the inner depth of the human soul, which is separated from the human being during heedlessness and connected to him during recollection. This is what the gnostics, the Illuminationists (أهل الإشراق), and their peers from other nations and sects say. Some formulated it in the manner of the gnostics, except that they stipulated godwariness (التقوى) and following the Sacred Law (الشرع) in knowledge and action, like a number of Muslims among our contemporaries and others, under the assumption that the condition of following the Sacred Law distinguishes them from the gnostics and Sufis. It was hidden from them that the gnostics preceded them in this stipulation, as witnessed by their extant authoritative books. Thus, the view is identical to what the Sufis said; the only difference between the two groups is in the manner of following and the identification of the meaning of "following." These people consider "following" to be a stage of pure adherence to the literal meanings (الجمود على الظواهر), so their path is a path born from the union of the paths of the Sufis and the Akhbaris (الأخبارية), among other formulations.

The doctrine of recollection (التذكر), if it does not intend to invalidate the recourse to logical and intellectual principles, is not devoid of a degree of correctness in general. For when a human being exists in his identity, he exists as one conscious of his essence, the powers of his essence, and his causes, knowing them with presential knowledge (علما حضوريا). He possesses powers that transform his presential knowledge into acquired knowledge (علم حصولي). No power exists as a principle of action except that it performs its function. Thus, the human being, at the beginning of his existence, possesses something of the sciences, even if they are posterior to him by nature, they are with him in time. Furthermore, the attainment of some sciences for the human being when he turns away from material attachments to some extent is something no one can deny.

However, if the doctrine of recollection is intended to invalidate the effect of recourse to logical and intellectual principles—in the sense that the arrangement of appropriate self-evident premises necessitates the human being's transition from potentiality to actuality regarding the knowledge that counts as their result, or in the sense that recollection, meaning returning to the soul through purgation (التخلية), dispenses with the human being's need to arrange scientific premises to attain results—then it is among the most absurd of views that yield no result.

As for the doctrine of recollection in the sense of it invalidating the recourse to logical and intellectual principles, it is refuted, firstly, by the fact that deep investigation into human sciences and knowledge shows that his judgmental knowledge (علومه التصديقية) depends on his conceptual knowledge (علومه التصورية), and conceptual sciences are limited to sensory sciences or what is abstracted from them in some way. Syllogism and experience have shown that he who lacks one of the senses lacks all the sciences that terminate in that sense, whether conceptual or judgmental, theoretical or self-evident. If the sciences existed for the human identity in actuality, the assumed loss would not affect that. To say that blindness, deafness, and the like are barriers to recollection is a retreat from the original doctrine—which is that recollection, meaning returning to the soul by turning away from material attachments, is beneficial for remembering the sought object upon the removal of heedlessness.

Secondly: recollection is only achieved by some individuals of this species, while the general population employs the method of synthesis and deduction for their vital objectives, deriving thousands upon thousands of sound results. This is the case in all sciences and crafts. To deny any of this is stubbornness (مكابرة), and to attribute it to chance is recklessness. Adopting this method is an innate (فطري) matter for the human being from which there is no escape. It is impossible for a species to be equipped with an innate, ontological apparatus (جهاز فطري تكويني) and then stumble in its operation and fail in its endeavor.

Thirdly: everything these people attain through what they call recollection returns, upon analysis, to premises arranged in a logical order, such that the matter of the result is disturbed by the disturbance of any of the established principles in its form or matter. Thus, they employ logical principles from where they do not perceive it. Constant chance and coincidence have no meaning. They must produce a correct "recollected" scientific form in which the principles of logic do not apply.

As for the doctrine of recollection in the sense of it dispensing with the recourse to logical principles—the gist of which returns to there being two paths: the path of logic and the path of recollection through following the Sacred Law, for example, and that the two paths are equal in reaching the truth, or that the path of recollection is better and more worthy because it always hits the mark due to its agreement with the word of the Infallible (المعصوم), unlike the path of logic and reason—this carries the danger of falling into error always or mostly.

In any case, the second objection raised against what preceded it applies here as well: for encompassing all the objectives of the Book and the Sunnah, and their symbols and secrets, given their wondrously vast scope, is not possible except for the few individuals who are deeply immersed in reflecting upon religious knowledge, with its wondrous interconnectedness and the profound overlap between its roots and branches, and what pertains to belief and what pertains to individual and social actions. It is impossible for a human being to be ontologically (تكوينا) tasked through his ontological equipment with what is beyond his capacity and ability, or to be so tasked legislatively (تشريعا). People are only required to understand the objectives of religion through the path familiar to them in the affairs of their individual and social lives, which is the arrangement of knowns to derive unknowns. What is known from the Sacred Law is one category of knowns, because the demonstration (البرهان) has established its truth.

It is strange that some of those who hold the doctrine of recollection made this very point a reason for preferring recollection over logic. He mentioned that if knowledge of actual realities were possible through the use of logic and philosophy—and it is not—it would only be possible for the likes of Aristotle and Ibn Sina among the unique masters of philosophy, and would not be possible for the general public. How then could the Lawgiver command the use of logic and philosophical principles as a path to attaining realities?

He did not realize that the objection is exactly reversed against him. If he replies that the use of recollection is easy for everyone according to their following [of the Law], it is replied that the use of logic, whether a little or a lot, is easy for everyone according to their aptitude for attaining realities. It is not necessary for everyone to reach the ultimate goal or to undertake what is beyond their capacity.

Secondly, the third previous objection applies to him: for these people employ the path of logic in all the objectives they present in the name of recollection, as mentioned before, even in the explanation they provided to invalidate the path of logic and establish the path of recollection. This alone is sufficient as corruption.

Thirdly: falling into error occurs, indeed it is prevalent, in the path of recollection they mentioned. For recollection, as they claimed, is the path that the righteous predecessors followed instead of the path of logic. Yet, disagreement and error have been reported among them in no small measure, such as a number of the companions of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) whom the Muslims agreed upon their knowledge and their following of the Book and the Sunnah, or whom the majority agreed upon their jurisprudence and justice. Likewise, a number of the companions of the Imams with these descriptions, such as Abu Hamza, Zurarah, Aban, Abu Khalid, the two Hishams, Mu'min al-Taq, the two Safwans, and others. The fundamental disagreements among them are famous and well-known. It is clear that among those who disagree, only one of them attains the truth. Similarly, the jurists and traditionists (المحدثون) among the ancients, such as al-Kulayni, al-Saduq, the Shaykh of the Sect [al-Tusi], al-Mufid, al-Murtada, and others (رضوان الله عليهم). So what is the advantage of recollection over logical reflection? It was necessary then to seek another criterion besides recollection to distinguish between truth and falsehood, and it is none other than logical reflection; thus, it is the reference and the refuge.

Fourthly: the summary of the argument is that if a human being holds fast to the coattails of the People of Infallibility and Purity, he will not fall into error. Its implication is what preceded: that the opinion taken from the Infallible in what was heard from him with certain hearing and whose meaning was known with certain knowledge is something in which no error occurs. This is something no one disputes.

In reality, what is heard from the Infallible or taken from him is the matter (مادة), not the recollection itself nor the logical thought. Then what follows it is: "This is what the Infallible sees, and everything he sees is truth, so this is truth." This is a syllogism with a certain result. As for any other form, such as the contents of solitary reports (أخبار الآحاد) or their like, which yield nothing but conjecture (الظن), that yields nothing, and there is no evidence for the authority of solitary reports in matters other than legal rulings except with agreement with the Book. Nor does conjecture obtain anything when knowledge to the contrary is assumed from a scientific proof.

**9 - The saying of some of them:** "Indeed, Allah, the Glorified, addressed us in His speech with what we are familiar with in the discourse circulating among us, and the order and composition known to the people of the language. The apparent meanings (ظاهر البيانات) comprise command, prohibition, promise, threat, stories, wisdom, exhortation, and arguing in the best manner. These are matters that do not require the learning of logic, philosophy, and the rest of what is the heritage of the disbelievers and polytheists and the way of the wrongdoers for their understanding and comprehension. He has forbidden us from their guardianship, from leaning toward them, from adopting their habits, and from following their paths. Thus, it is only for the one who believes in Allah and His Messenger to take the apparent meanings of the religious explanations and stop at what ordinary understanding receives from those apparent meanings, without interpreting them or exceeding them to anything else." This is the view of the Hashwiyya (الحشوية), the Mushabbiha (المشبهة), and a number of the people of Hadith.

This is false. As for its form (الهيئة), logical principles have been employed in it, while the intent was to prohibit their very use. The one who says that the Quran guides to the use of logical principles did not say that every Muslim must learn logic; rather, the use itself is something from which there is no escape. The likeness of these people in this saying of theirs is but the likeness of one who says: "The Quran only intends to guide us to the objectives of religion, so we have no need to learn the language which is the heritage of the people of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية)." Just as this speech has no weight, since language is a path needed by the human being in the stage of communication by nature, and Allah, the Glorified, has used it in His Book and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in his Sunnah, likewise there is no meaning to the objection against logic, since it is a mental path needed by the human being in the stage of intellection by nature, and Allah, the Glorified, has used it in His Book and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in his Sunnah.

As for its matter (المادة), intellectual matters have been taken into it, except that it was fallacious in terms of equating the apparent meaning of the speech with the instances (المصاديق) to which the meanings and concepts apply. What the Muslim who believes in the Book of Allah must understand from things like knowledge, power, life, hearing, sight, speech, volition, and will, for example, is to understand meanings that contrast with ignorance, incapacity, death, deafness, blindness, and the like. But as for establishing for Allah, the Glorified, a knowledge like our knowledge, a power like our power, a life like our life, and a hearing, sight, speech, volition, and will likewise—he has no right to do that, neither by the Book, nor the Sunnah, nor reason. A portion of the discourse related to this chapter has already preceded in the discussion of the Clear (المحكم) and the Ambiguous (المتشابه) in the third volume of the book.

**10 - The saying of some of them:** "The proof for the authority of the premises upon which intellectual arguments are based is nothing but the intellectual premise stating the necessity of following the intellectual judgment. In other words, there is no proof for the judgment of reason except reason itself, and this is an explicit circularity (دور مصرح). Thus, in disputed matters, there is no escape from referring to the word of the Infallible, whether a prophet or an imam, without imitation (تقليد)." This is the most absurd skepticism raised in this chapter. It was intended to fortify a structure, but it resulted in its destruction. For the claimant invalidated the judgment of reason by what he assumed to be explicit circularity, then when he returned to the judgment of the Sacred Law, he was forced either to argue for it by the judgment of reason—which is circularity—or by the judgment of the Sacred Law—which is circularity. Thus, he remained bewildered, circling between two circularities. Unless he returns to imitation, which is a second bewilderment.

The matter was confused for him in attaining the meaning of "the necessity of following the judgment of reason." If what is meant by the necessity of following the judgment of reason is that which contrasts with prohibition and permissibility, and whose violation entails blame or punishment—similar to the necessity of following a sincere advisor, or the necessity of justice in judgment and the like—then this is a judgment of practical reason (العقل العملي), and we have no dispute regarding it. But if what is meant by the necessity of following is that the human being is compelled to assent to the result when it is argued for with scientific premises and a correct scientific form along with complete conceptualization of the terms of the propositions, then this is something the human being witnesses by intuition (بالوجدان). There is no meaning then to asking reason for proof, for the authority of its proof is due to the self-evidence of its authority. This is like all other self-evident truths (البديهيات), for the proof for every self-evident truth is only itself, meaning it is independent of proof.
