**11 - The saying of some of them:** "The ultimate goal sought by logic is to obtain the fixed essences (الماهيات) of things, and to obtain results through universal, permanent, and fixed premises (المقدمات الكلية الدائمة الثابتة). Yet it has been established by modern scientific research today that there is neither universal, nor permanent, nor fixed in external reality or the mind; rather, things flow under the law of general transformation without anything specific remaining in a fixed, permanent, or universal state."

This is false because logical principles were employed in it, in both form and matter, as is apparent to whoever reflects upon it. Furthermore, the objector intends by this very objection to conclude that ancient logic is not correct at all, which is a universal, permanent, and fixed result comprising fixed concepts; otherwise, it would yield him nothing. Thus, the objection invalidates itself.

Perhaps we have exceeded the condition of this book in preferring brevity whenever possible, so let us return to where we were: The Noble Quran guides the intellects to use what they were naturally disposed (فطرت) to use and to follow what they are familiar with and recognize by their nature, which is the arrangement of knowns to derive unknowns. What the intellects are naturally disposed toward is to use real, certain premises to derive judgmental information regarding reality, which is demonstration (البرهان); and to use, in what pertains to action—such as happiness and misery, good and evil, benefit and harm, and what ought to be chosen and preferred and what ought not, which are conventional matters (الأمور الاعتبارية)—widely-known or conceded premises, which is dialectic (الجدل); and to use, in cases of suspected good and evil, conjectural premises to produce guidance and leading toward a suspected good, or deterrence from a suspected evil, which is exhortation (العظة). The Exalted said: **ادْعُ إِلى سَبِيلِ رَبِّكَ بِالْحِكْمَةِ وَالْمَوْعِظَةِ الْحَسَنَةِ وَجادِلْهُمْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ** (Invite to the way of your Lord with wisdom and good advice and dispute with them in a manner that is best. [16:125]). It is apparent that what is meant by wisdom (الحكمة) is demonstration, as indicated by its being contrasted with good advice and dispute.

If you say: The path of logical reflection is something the disbeliever and the believer are both capable of, and it is possible for the profligate and the godwary. What then is the meaning of His, the Exalted, denial of pleasing knowledge and correct recollection to those other than the people of godwariness and following, as in His saying: **وَما يَتَذَكَّرُ إِلَّا مَنْ يُنِيبُ** (and none takes admonition except him who returns penitently [40:13]), and His saying: **وَمَنْ يَتَّقِ اللهَ يَجْعَلْ لَهُ مَخْرَجاً** (And whoever is wary of Allah, He shall make for him a way out [65:2]), and His saying: **فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْ مَنْ تَوَلَّى عَنْ ذِكْرِنا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا الْحياةَ الدُّنْيا ذلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ ضَلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ اهْتَدى** (So avoid those who turn away from Our remembrance and desire nothing but the life of the world. That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge. Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way, and He knows best those who are guided. [53:29-30])? And the narrations stating that beneficial knowledge is not attained except through righteous action are numerous and widespread.

I say: The Book and the Sunnah's consideration of godwariness (التقوى) alongside knowledge is beyond doubt. However, this is not to make godwariness—or godwariness accompanied by recollection—an independent path for attaining realities beyond the innate intellectual path (الطريق الفكري الفطري) that the human being engages in by necessity. For if the matter were so, all the arguments (الاحتجاجات) mentioned in the Book against the disbelievers, polytheists, and people of profligacy and immorality—who do not follow the truth and do not know what godwariness and recollection are—would be futile, as they would have no way, in this state, to perceive the sought objective. And assuming their state changes, the argument with them becomes futile. Similar to this are the arguments mentioned in the Sunnah with various stray sects and groups.

Rather, the consideration of godwariness is to return the perceiving human soul to its innate uprightness (استقامتها الفطرية). To clarify: the human being, in terms of his physicality, is composed of opposing powers—bestial (بهيمية) and predatory (سبعية)—whose origin is the elemental body. Each of them performs its own specific conscious action without being linked to other powers in a way that observes their state in its action, except by way of resistance and opposition. The appetite for food impels the human being to eat and drink without being limited by any boundary or measure from the side of this power, unless the stomach, for example, resists because it can only hold a limited amount, or the jaw resists due to fatigue and exhaustion affecting its muscle from chewing if he eats excessively, and similar things. These are matters we witness in ourselves constantly.

When this is the case, the human being's inclination toward one of the powers, his indulgence in obeying its commands, and his responding to what it impels him toward, necessitates the tyranny of the obeyed power and the oppression of the power opposing it—an oppression that might reach the point of annihilation or nearly so. Thus, indulgence in the appetite for food or the appetite for sex distracts the human being from all the important tasks of life, such as earning, social interaction, organizing the home, raising children, and all other individual and social duties that must be performed. Similar to this is indulgence in obeying other appetitive and irascible powers. This too is something we continue to witness in ourselves and others throughout the days of life.

In this excess and deficiency lies the destruction of humanity. For the human being is the soul that has harnessed these different powers, and his only business is to drive the collective of powers with their actions toward his happiness in the life of this world and the Hereafter, which is nothing but a life of knowledge and perfection. Thus, he has no choice but to give each of the powers its share such that it does not conflict with the other powers nor is it completely abolished.

The meaning of humanity is not completed for the human being unless he balances his different powers—a balancing that brings each of them to the middle of the path legislated for it. The habit of moderation (ملكة الاعتدال) in each of the powers is what we call its virtuous character (خلقها الفاضل), such as wisdom (الحكمة), courage (الشجاعة), temperance (العفة), and others; and justice (العدالة) encompasses them all.

There is no doubt that the human being only obtains these ideas he possesses and expands his human knowledge and sciences through these conscious powers suggesting their actions and requirements. This means that the human being, at the beginning of his existence, is empty-handed of these vast sciences and knowledge until his internal powers perceive their needs and suggest to him what they desire and seek. These initial perceptions are the principles of human sciences; then the human being continues to generalize, specify, synthesize, and analyze until the matter of human thoughts is completed for him.

From here, the intelligent person surmises that the human being's immersion in obeying one of his opposing powers and his extravagance in responding to what it suggests necessitates his deviation in his thoughts and knowledge, by making everything this power validates dominate over the judgments and thoughts provided by others, and by his heedlessness of what others require.

Experience confirms this. For this deviation is what we witness in extravagant, affluent individuals who are allies of appetite, and in the rebellious, tyrannical wrongdoers who corrupt the affairs of life in human society. For these people, plunged into the depths of appetites, devoted to the pleasures of drinking, listening, and union, can hardly reflect upon the duties of humanity and the important matters for which heroic men compete. The spirit of appetite has seeped into their sitting, standing, gathering, parting, and so on. Likewise, the arrogant tyrants, hard of heart, cannot conceive of kindness, compassion, mercy, humility, and self-abasement even where it is required. Their life represents the vile state they are in through all its manifestations: speech, silence, looking, lowering the gaze, approaching, and turning away. All of these are traversers of the path of error in their sciences; each group is bent upon the distorted and deviated sciences and thoughts they attain related to what they possess, heedless of what lies beyond it—and beyond it lie beneficial sciences and true human knowledge. Thus, true knowledge and beneficial sciences are not completed for the human being unless his character is reformed and the valuable human virtues—which is godwariness (التقوى)—are completed for him.

It has thus been established that righteous deeds are what preserve good character, and good character is what preserves true knowledge, beneficial sciences, and correct thoughts. There is no good in knowledge without action.

Although we have conducted this discussion in a scientific and ethical manner due to the pressing need for clarification, it is what Allah, the Exalted, summarized in a single word when He said: **وَاقْصِدْ فِي مَشْيِكَ** (and be modest in your bearing [31:19]), for it is a metaphor for taking the middle path of moderation in the course of life. And He said: **إِنْ تَتَّقُوا اللهَ يَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ فُرْقاناً** (If you are wary of Allah, He shall appoint a criterion for you [8:29]). And He said: **وَتَزَوَّدُوا فَإِنَّ خَيْرَ الزَّادِ التَّقْوى وَاتَّقُونِ يا أُولِي الْأَلْبابِ** (And take provision, for indeed the best provision is godwariness. And be wary of Me, O you who possess intellects! [2:197]), meaning: because you possess intellects, you need godwariness in the operation of your intellects—and Allah knows best. And the Exalted said: **وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاها وَقَدْ خابَ مَنْ دَسَّاها** (By the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its wickedness and its godwariness, one who purifies it is certainly felicitous, and one who betrays it has certainly failed. [91:7-10]). And He said: **وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ** (and be wary of Allah, so that you may be felicitous. [3:130]).

From another path: The Exalted said: **فَخَلَفَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ أَضاعُوا الصَّلاةَ وَاتَّبَعُوا الشَّهَواتِ فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا إِلَّا مَنْ تابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً** (Then there succeeded them an inheritance who neglected the prayer and followed appetites. So they will soon encounter [the reward of] perversity, except those who repent, believe, and act righteously. [19:59-60]). He mentioned that following appetites leads to perversity (الغي). And the Exalted said: **سَأَصْرِفُ عَنْ آياتِيَ الَّذِينَ يَتَكَبَّرُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِّ وَإِنْ يَرَوْا كُلَّ آيَةٍ لا يُؤْمِنُوا بِها وَإِنْ يَرَوْا سَبِيلَ الرُّشْدِ لا يَتَّخِذُوهُ سَبِيلاً وَإِنْ يَرَوْا سَبِيلَ الغَيِّ يَتَّخِذُوهُ سَبِيلاً ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا وَكانُوا عَنْها غافِلِينَ** (I shall turn away from My signs those who are arrogant in the earth unduly; [even] if they see every sign, they will not believe in it, and if they see the way of rectitude, they will not take it as [their] way, and if they see the way of error, they will take it as [their] way. That is because they denied Our signs and were oblivious of them. [7:146]). He mentioned that the captives of the irascible powers are prevented from following the truth and are driven to the path of perversity; then He mentioned that this is due to their heedlessness of the truth. And the Exalted said: **وَلَقَدْ ذَرَأْنا لِجَهَنَّمَ كَثِيراً مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ لا يَفْقَهُونَ بِها وَلَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ لا يُبْصِرُونَ بِها وَلَهُمْ آذانٌ لا يَسْمَعُونَ بِها أُولئِكَ كَالْأَنْعامِ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْغافِلُونَ** (Certainly We have winnowed for hell many of the jinn and humans: they have hearts with which they do not understand, they have eyes with which they do not see, and they have ears with which they do not hear. They are like cattle; rather, they are more astray. It is they who are the heedless. [7:179]). He mentioned that these heedless ones are only heedless of the realities of knowledge (حقائق المعارف) that belong to the human being; thus, their hearts, eyes, and ears are isolated from attaining what the human being, felicitous in his humanity, attains. They only attain through them what cattle attain, or what is more astray than cattle—which are the thoughts that the grazing beasts and predatory animals validate, incline toward, and are familiar with.

It has appeared from all that preceded that the Noble Quran only stipulated godwariness (التقوى) in reflection, recollection, and intellection, and paired knowledge with action to obtain the uprightness of thought, the correctness of knowledge, and its purity from the taints of animalistic illusions and satanic suggestions.

Yes, there is a Quranic reality here that cannot be denied: that the human being's entry into the enclosure of Divine Guardianship (الولاية الإلهية) and his proximity to the presence of Sanctity and Grandeur opens for him a door to the kingdom of the heavens and the earth (ملكوت السماوات والأرض), through which he witnesses what is hidden from others of the greatest signs of Allah and the inextinguishable lights of His Might. Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) said: "Were it not that the devils hover around the hearts of the children of Adam, they would have seen the kingdom of the heavens and the earth." And in what the majority narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), he said: "Were it not for the excess in your speech and the confusion in your hearts, you would see what I see and hear what I hear." And the Exalted has said: **وَالَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا فِينا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنا وَإِنَّ اللهَ لَمَعَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** (As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them in Our ways, and Allah is indeed with the virtuous. [29:69]). This is also indicated by the apparent meaning of His, the Exalted, saying: **وَاعْبُدْ رَبَّكَ حَتَّى يَأْتِيَكَ الْيَقِينُ** (and worship your Lord until certainty comes to you. [15:99]), where He made certainty (اليقين) a branch of worship. And the Exalted said: **وَكَذلِكَ نُرِي إِبْراهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ** (Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who possess certainty. [6:75]), linking the description of possessing certainty to the witnessing of the dominion (الملكوت). And the Exalted said: **كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ ثُمَّ لَتَرَوُنَّها عَيْنَ الْيَقِينِ** (No indeed! Were you to know with certain knowledge, you would surely see hell. Then you will surely see it with the eye of certainty. [102:5-7]). And the Exalted said: **إِنَّ كِتابَ الْأَبْرارِ لَفِي عِلِّيِّينَ وَما أَدْراكَ ما عِلِّيُّونَ كِتابٌ مَرْقُومٌ يَشْهَدُهُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ** (Indeed the record of the pious is in Illiyyun. And what will show you what is Illiyyun? It is a transcribed record, witnessed by those brought near [to Allah]. [83:18-21]). One should seek the exhaustive discussion of this meaning in what will follow of the commentary on His, the Exalted, saying: **إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ** (Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle... [5:55]) and in His, the Exalted, saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ** (O you who have faith! Take care of your own souls... [5:105]).

The establishment of this reality does not contradict what we have presented—that the Noble Quran supports the path of innate reflection (التفكر الفطري) upon which the human being was naturally disposed and upon which the structure of human life was built. For this is a non-discursive path and a divine gift (موهبة إلهية) with which He singles out whom He wills of His servants, and the end is for the godwary.

## (Historical Discussion)

We shall look briefly into the history of Islamic thought and the path taken by the Islamic nation across its various groups and schools. We shall not lean toward any of the schools by validating or invalidating; rather, we shall present the occurring events to the logic of the Quran and judge them by their agreement or disagreement. As for what a supporter boasts of or an opponent excuses, we have no business diving into their roots and origins, for that is another path of research, sectarian or otherwise.

The Noble Quran, with its logic, addresses all affairs of human life in its legislated way without being restricted by any limit or conditioned by any condition. It judges the human being individually or socially, young or old, male or female, the white and the black, the Arab and the non-Arab, the city-dweller and the nomad, the scholar and the ignorant, the present and the absent, in whatever time and in whatever place. It intervenes in every affair of his—belief, character, or action—without doubt.

Thus, the Quran has contact with all sciences and crafts related to the aspects of human life. It is clear and manifest through its verses—which urge contemplation (التدبر), reflection (التفكر), recollection (التذكر), and intellection (التعقل)—that it strongly encourages the pursuit of knowledge and the rejection of ignorance in everything related to the celestial and terrestrial realms, plants, animals, and humans, the parts of our world and what lies beyond it of angels, devils, the Tablet (اللوح), the Pen (القلم), and so on, so that it may be a means to the knowledge of Allah, the Glorified. It also encourages what relates in some way to the happiness of social human life, such as ethics, laws, rights, and social rulings.

You have learned that it supports the innate path of reflection (الطريق الفطري من التفكر) to which the primordial nature (الفطرة) calls with an inescapable, necessary call, according to the truth of what the primordial nature calls for in terms of logical progression.

The Quran itself employs these logical arts of demonstration (برهان), dialectic (جدل), and exhortation (موعظة), and calls the nation it guides to follow it in that. Thus, they should employ demonstration in what pertains to external realities outside the domain of action, and argue using conceded premises (المسلمات) in other matters or what serves as a lesson.

The Quran, in explaining its objectives, considered the Prophetic Sunnah and designated for them the exemplar in the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Thus, they used to memorize from him and imitate his scientific conduct as a student imitates his teacher in scientific behavior.

The people during the era of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—and we mean the days of his residence in Medina—were new to Islamic teaching. Their state was similar to that of ancient man in the codification of sciences and crafts; they engaged in scientific inquiries in a simple, non-technical manner, despite their concern for attainment and refinement. They first focused on memorizing and reciting the Quran, and memorizing the Hadith from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) without writing it down, and transmitting it. They had some theological debates (مطارحات كلامية) among themselves and arguments with some followers of foreign religions, especially the Jews and Christians, due to the presence of generations of them in the Peninsula, Abyssinia, and the Levant. From here began the emergence of the science of theology (علم الكلام). They also engaged in the narration of poetry, which was an Arabic tradition that Islam did not emphasize; the Book did not praise poetry or poets with a single word, nor did the Sunnah exaggerate its importance.

Then, when the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) passed away, the matter of the Caliphate occurred as is well known, and the disagreement that arose then added a new door to the existing ones.

The Quran was collected during the time of the first Caliph after the Battle of Yamama and the martyrdom of a group of reciters therein.

Matters remained thus during the era of his caliphate—which was approximately two years—then during the era of the second Caliph.

Although Islam's fame spread and its scope expanded through the great conquests granted to the Muslims during his era, the engagement with them hindered them from deep reflection upon the connections between sciences and seeking advancement in their levels; or perhaps they did not see a need for expansion and elaboration given the scientific level they possessed.

Knowledge and its merit are not a sensory matter that one nation recognizes from another unless it is linked to a craft, whereby its effect appears to the senses and the general public recognizes it.

These successive, abundant conquests awakened the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance from their vanity and pride after they had been settled by Prophetic upbringing. The spirit of the superior, tyrannical nations began to seep into them and gradually take hold of them. This is witnessed by the prevalence of dividing the Muslim nation then into Arabs and clients (الموالي), and the conduct of Mu'awiya—who was the governor of the Levant then—among the Muslims with a royal, Caesarean conduct, and many other things mentioned by history regarding the Muslim armies. These are psychological states that have an impact on the scientific course, especially the Quranic teachings.

As for what they had of the current scientific course, the engagement with the Quran remained as it was, and it had become multiple codices (مصاحف) attributed to Zayd, Ubayy, Ibn Mas'ud, and others.

As for Hadith, it flourished clearly, and transmission and recording increased to the point where Umar forbade some companions from narrating due to the abundance of what they reported. A number of the People of the Book had entered Islam, and the traditionists took from them a great deal of the reports of their books and the stories of their prophets and nations, mixing them with the Hadith they had memorized from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Fabrication (الوضع) and interpolation (الدس) began to circulate in the Hadith. Today, in the disconnected reports (الأحاديث المقطوعة) narrated from the companions and their transmitters in the first century, there is a great deal of this which the Quran refutes by its literal wording.

The overall cause for this consists of three matters:

1 — The high status that people believed belonged to the companionship of the Prophet and the memorization of Hadith from him, and the honor of the companions and their transmitters among the people, and their veneration of their affair. This invited people to take and increase (even from the Muslims of the People of the Book) and created intense competition among the bearers of Hadith in attaining precedence and pride.

2 — Their intense eagerness to memorize and transmit Hadith prevented them from scrutinizing it and reflecting upon its meaning, especially in presenting it to the Book of Allah, which is the foundation upon which the structure of religion is built and from which its branches are derived. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had enjoined them to do so in his sound saying: "Fabricators against me will increase..." and other reports.

Through this, an opportunity arose for fabricated Hadith to circulate among them regarding the attributes of Allah, His names, and His acts; slips attributed to the noble prophets; ugly distortions attributed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); myths regarding creation and origination; stories of past nations; and the corruption of the Quran, among other things that are no less than what the Torah and the Gospel contain of this sort.

At that point, the Quran and Hadith divided precedence and practice: formal precedence belonged to the Quran, while taking and acting belonged to the Hadith. Thus, the Quran was soon abandoned in practice. This practice—which is the neglect of presenting Hadith to the Quran—has continued to run among the nation in practice until today, even if they deny it in speech: **وَقالَ الرَّسُولُ يا رَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِي اتَّخَذُوا هذَا الْقُرْآنَ مَهْجُوراً** (And the Apostle will say, ‘O my Lord! Indeed my people consigned this Quran to oblivion.’ [25:30]), except for rare individuals.

This very negligence is one of the reasons for the persistence of many ancient national myths among the Islamic nations after their entry into Islam; and disease breeds disease.

3 — What occurred in the matter of the Caliphate after the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) necessitated a disagreement in the opinions of the general Muslims regarding his household (أهل بيته). Some were devoted to them and enamored by them, while others were indifferent to them, not caring for their affair or their status in the knowledge of the Quran, or were hateful and antagonistic toward them. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had enjoined them—in what no Muslim doubts its soundness and indication—to learn from them and not to teach them, for they are more knowledgeable than them in the Book of Allah. He mentioned to them that they would never err in its interpretation nor mistake its understanding.

He said in the mutawatir Hadith of the Two Weighty Things (حديث الثقلين): "Indeed, I am leaving among you the two weighty things: the Book of Allah and my progeny (عترتي)—and they shall never separate until they return to me at the Pond..."

In some of its chains: "Do not teach them, for they are more knowledgeable than you."

And he said in the widespread (المستفيض) of his speech: "Whoever interprets the Quran by his own opinion, let him take his seat in the Fire." This has already been discussed in the research on the Clear and the Ambiguous in the third volume of the book.

This was the greatest breach by which the knowledge of the Quran and the path of reflection to which it invites were damaged. A witness to this indifference is the small number of Hadith narrated from them (عليه‌السلام). For if you reflect upon the status and honor that the science of Hadith had during the era of the Caliphs, and the intense greed and eagerness people had for taking it, and then you count what was narrated in that regard from Ali, al-Hasan, and al-Husayn—especially what was narrated from that in the interpretation of the Quran—you would see something amazing: as for the companions, they narrated almost nothing from Ali (عليه‌السلام). As for the followers (التابعون), what they narrated from him—if counted—does not reach a hundred narrations for the entire Quran.

As for al-Hasan (عليه‌السلام), perhaps what is narrated from him does not reach ten. As for al-Husayn, nothing significant was narrated from him. Some have estimated the narrations regarding interpretation to be seventeen thousand (1) Hadith from the path of the majority alone, and this proportion exists in the narrations of jurisprudence (الفقه) as well (2).

Is this because they abandoned the People of the Household and turned away from their Hadith? Or because they took from them and increased, but then it was hidden and forgotten during the Umayyad state due to the Umayyads' deviation from them? I do not know.

However, the isolation of Ali and his non-participation in the collection of the Quran first and last, and the history of the lives of al-Hasan and al-Husayn (عليه‌السلام), supports the first of the two possibilities.

The matter of his [Ali's] Hadith reached the point where some denied that what the book *Nahj al-Balagha* contains of his brilliant sermons was his speech; yet regarding the likes of the "Mutilated Sermon" (الخطبة البتراء) of Ziyad ibn Abih and the wine-poetry of Yazid, hardly two people disagree!

The People of the Household remained oppressed and their Hadith abandoned until the two Imams, Muhammad ibn Ali al-Baqir and Ja'far ibn Muhammad al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), arose during a period like a truce between the Umayyad state and the Abbasid state. They then clarified what had been lost of their fathers' Hadith and renewed what had been erased and effaced of their traces.

However, their Hadith and that of others among their fathers and sons from the Imams of the People of the Household also did not escape the intruder and was not pure from interpolation and fabrication, just like the Hadith of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). They mentioned this in the explicit of their speech, and they named men among the fabricators such as Mughira ibn Sa'id, Ibn Abi al-Khattab, and others. Some of the Imams denied many narrations reported from them and from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and they commanded their companions and their Shi'a to present the Hadith narrated from them to the Quran, taking what agreed with it and leaving what contradicted it.

However, the people—except for a few individuals among them—did not apply this principle in practice regarding the narrations (الأحاديث) of the People of the House (أهل البيت) (peace be upon them), especially in fields other than jurisprudence (الفقه). The path they followed in this regard was the same path followed by the majority (الجمهور) concerning the narrations of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

(1) Al-Suyuti mentioned this in *al-Itqan*, noting that he enumerated the narrations in his exegesis (تفسير) titled *Tarjuman al-Quran* and its abridgment titled *al-Durr al-Manthur*.

(2) Some researchers mentioned that they found two narrations reported from al-Husayn (peace be upon him) within the jurisprudential reports.

This matter was taken to such an extreme that a group went so far as to deny the authoritative status (حجية) of the apparent meanings (ظواهر) of the Book, while upholding the authority of works like *Misbah al-Shari'ah*, *Fiqh al-Rida*, and *Jami' al-Akhbar*! The excess reached a point where some stated that a narration (الحديث) can interpret the Quran even if it contradicts its explicit indication (صريح دلالته). This parallels what some of the majority (الجمهور) mentioned: that a report (الخبر) can abrogate (ينسخ) the Book. Perhaps the situation of the Ummah as it appears to other researchers—as some have noted—is that: "The Sunnis took the Book and left the Progeny (العترة), which led to them leaving the Book because of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: 'Indeed, the two shall never separate.' And the Shi'a took the Progeny and left the Book, which led to them leaving the Progeny because of his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: 'Indeed, the two shall never separate.' Thus, the Ummah has left both the Quran and the Progeny—the Book and the Sunnah—together."

This methodology followed regarding narrations is one of the factors that contributed to the severing of the link between the Islamic sciences—both the religious and the literary sciences—and the Quran. This occurred despite the fact that all of them are like branches and fruits of this "goodly tree whose root is firmly fixed and whose branch is in the sky, yielding its fruit at all times by the leave of its Lord." Indeed, if you reflect upon the state of these sciences, you will find they have been organized in a manner that they have no need for the Quran at all. It is possible for a student to learn them all—morphology (الصرف), grammar (النحو), rhetoric (البيان), linguistics (اللغة), the science of narrations (الحديث), the study of narrators (الرجال), the principles of narration (الدراية), jurisprudence (الفقه), and legal theory (الأصول)—reaching the end of these studies, becoming proficient, and then attaining the rank of *ijtihad* (الاجتهاد) — *the process of deriving legal rulings from original sources* — and mastery therein, without ever having read the Quran or even touching a copy of the Scripture (المصحف). Thus, in reality, nothing remains for the Quran except its recitation to earn spiritual reward, or its use as an amulet (تميمة) for children to protect them from the calamities of time! Take heed, then, if you are among those capable of reflection.

Let us return to our subject: the state of inquiry regarding the Quran and narrations during the era of Umar was as you have heard. The scope of theological (كلامية) inquiries expanded during this era because the vast conquests naturally led to the intermingling of Muslims with other nations and followers of various religions and sects (الملل والنحل), among whom were scholars, rabbis (الأحبار), bishops (الأساقفة), and patriarchs (البطارقة) who researched religions and schools of thought. Thus, the beacon of theology (الكلام) was raised, though it had not yet been formally compiled, as the works attributed to it are only mentioned in the biographies of those who came after this era.

The situation remained thus during the era of Uthman, despite the people's uprising against the Caliphate; he was only successful in gathering the codices (المصاحف) and agreeing upon a single codex.

Then the matter continued as such during the caliphate of Ali (peace be upon him) and his preoccupation with rectifying the corruption that had afflicted the Muslim society due to internal disputes and the successive wars that followed.

However, he (peace be upon him) established the science of grammar (علم النحو) and dictated its universal principles (كلياته) to Abu al-Aswad al-Du'ali, one of his companions, commanding him to collect the particular (جزئيات) rules. Beyond that, he was only able to deliver expositions through sermons and narrations containing the foundational elements of religious knowledge (المعارف الدينية) and the most precious Quranic secrets. Furthermore, he engaged in theological disputations (احتجاجات كلامية) that are preserved in the compendiums of narrations.

The situation regarding the Quran and narrations remained thus during the era of Mu'awiyah and the Umayyads and Abbasids after him, until approximately the beginning of the fourth century of the Hijrah, which marks the end of the era of the Twelve Imams according to the Shi'a. No significant development occurred in the method of researching the Quran and narrations, except for what happened during Mu'awiyah's era regarding the exertion of effort to suppress the mention of the People of the House (أهل البيت) (peace be upon them), efface their legacy, and fabricate narrations (وضع الأحاديث). The religious government had transformed into an absolute monarchy (سلطنة استبدادية), and the Islamic Sunnah was changed into imperial rule. There was also the command of Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz to record the narrations, whereas the traditionists (المحدثون) had previously dealt with narrations through oral transmission and memorization without being bound by writing.

During this period, Arabic literature (الأدب العربي) flourished to its utmost extent. This began from the time of Mu'awiyah, who went to great lengths to promote poetry, followed by the Umayyads and then the Abbasids. Hundreds or thousands of dinars would sometimes be spent for a single verse of poetry or a literary nuance. People devoted themselves to poetry and its transmission, as well as the history and battles of the Arabs (أيام العرب), earning significant wealth through this. The Umayyads benefited from its promotion and the expenditure of wealth on it to strengthen their position against the Banu Hashim, as did the Abbasids later against the Banu Fatima. Similarly, they went to great lengths in honoring scholars to use them to influence the people and make them carry out whatever they desired and decreed.

The influence of poetry and literature in the scholarly community reached such a point that you see many scholars citing a poet's verse or a common proverb as evidence in intellectual issues or scientific inquiries, making it the final judgment. Often, they would build theoretical objectives upon linguistic issues, or at the very least, engage in a linguistic investigation of the subject's name before entering the actual inquiry. All of these matters had deep effects on the logic of researchers and their scholarly trajectory.

In those days, theological inquiry (البحث الكلامي) flourished, and books and treatises were written on it. It was not long before they split into two great factions: the Ash'arites (الأشاعرة) and the Mu'tazilites (المعتزلة). The foundations of their views existed during the time of the Caliphs, and even during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), as evidenced by the reported disputations of Ali (peace be upon him) regarding compulsion (الجبر), delegation (التفويض), predestination (القدر), capacity (الاستطاعة), and other issues, as well as what has been narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding that.(1) The two groups became distinct during this time by the differentiation of their methodologies: the Mu'tazilites prioritized what the independent intellect (العقل) determines over the religious apparent meanings (الظواهر الدينية), such as the doctrine of rational beauty and ugliness (الحسن والقبح العقليين), the impossibility of choosing one of two equal options without a preponderant factor (الترجيح من غير مرجح), the impossibility of imposing a duty beyond one's capacity (التكليف بما لا يطاق), capacity, delegation, and so on. The Ash'arites, on the other hand, prioritized the apparent meanings over the judgment of the intellect, asserting the negation of rational beauty and ugliness, the permissibility of choosing without a preponderant factor, the negation of capacity, the doctrine of compulsion, the pre-eternity of the Word of Allah (قدم كلام الله), and other matters mentioned in their books.

Then they organized the discipline, established technical terminologies (الاصطلاحات), and added issues to counter the philosophers in metaphysical inquiries (المباحث المعنوية) regarding general matters (الأمور العامة). This occurred after the translation of philosophical books into Arabic and the spread of their study among Muslims. The matter is not as some have mentioned—that theology (التكلم) appeared or branched out in Islam into Mu'tazilism and Ash'arism only after the transfer of philosophy to the Arabs; the existence of most of their issues and views in the narrations prior to that proves otherwise.

The Mu'tazilites continued to increase in number and grow in power and prestige from their first appearance until the early Abbasid era (the beginning of the third century AH). Then they began to follow the path of decline and fall until the Ayyubid kings eradicated them, and they became extinct. During their time and after them, countless people were killed for the crime of Mu'tazilism, known only to Allah, the Glorified. At that point, the atmosphere of theological inquiry became clear for the Ash'arites without opposition, and they delved deeply into it after their jurists (فقهاؤهم) had initially considered it a sin. Ash'arism has remained prevalent among them to this day.

The Shi'a also had a long-standing involvement in theology (التكلم). Their first emergence in theology was after the passing of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Most of them were from the Companions (الصحابة), such as Salman, Abu Dharr, al-Miqdad, Ammar, Amr ibn al-Hamiq, and others, and from the Followers (التابعين), such as Rashid, Kumayl, Maytham, and other Alids whom the Umayyads eradicated. Then they became firmly rooted and their cause grew strong again

(1) Such as the saying of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in what is narrated from him: **لا جبر ولا تفويض بل أمر بين أمرين** — "There is neither compulsion nor delegation, but a matter between the two matters," and his saying: **القدرية مجوس هذه الأمة** — "The Qadariyyah are the Magians of this Ummah."

during the time of the two Imams: al-Baqir and al-Sadiq (peace be upon them). They took up research and the authorship of books and treatises, and they continued to strive under the oppression and persecution of governments until they were granted some security during the Buyid state (1). Then they were suppressed again until the situation cleared for them with the emergence of the Safavid state in Iran (2), and they have continued as such until today.

The character of their theological inquiry was more similar to the Mu'tazilites than the Ash'arites. For this reason, some views sometimes became conflated, such as the doctrine of beauty and ugliness, the issue of choosing without a preponderant factor, the issue of predestination, and the issue of delegation. Also for this reason, the matter became confused for some people, who considered the two groups—the Shi'a and the Mu'tazilites—as having a single methodology in theological inquiry, like two racing horses. This is an error, for the principles narrated from the Imams of the People of the House (أهل البيت) (peace be upon them)—which are authoritative for the group—do not align with the Mu'tazilite taste in any way.

In short, the art of theology (فن الكلام) is a noble discipline that defends the true religious knowledge (المعارف الحقة الدينية). However, the Muslim theologians erred in their method of inquiry; they did not distinguish between intellectual judgments (الأحكام العقلية) and they conflated the truth (الحق) with the accepted (المقبول), as will be clarified to some extent later.

During this period of time, the sciences of the ancients—logic (المنطق), mathematics (الرياضيات), natural sciences (الطبيعيات), metaphysics (الإلهيات), medicine (الطب), and practical wisdom (الحكمة العملية)—were translated into Arabic. A portion was translated during the Umayyad era and then completed in the early Abbasid era. They translated hundreds of books from Greek, Roman, Indian, Persian, and Syriac into Arabic. People turned to studying various sciences and soon became independent in their thought, composing books and treatises on them. This used to enrage the scholars of the time, especially what they witnessed of the open display of atheists (الملاحدة) from the materialists (الدهرية), naturalists (الطبيعية), Manichaeans (المانوية), and others against the established matters of religion, and the attacks of the Muslim philosophizers (المتفلسفون) against religion and its people, treating the foundations of Islam and the pure landmarks of the Shari'ah with contempt and derision (and there is no disease like ignorance).

Among the things that enraged them most was what they heard from them regarding issues built upon premises (أصول موضوعة) taken from astronomy (الهيئة) and natural sciences, such as the Ptolemaic celestial spheres, their being a fifth nature, the impossibility of their tearing or mending (الخرق والالتيام), the pre-eternity of the spheres and celestial bodies in their individual identities (بالشخص), the pre-eternity of the elements in their species (بالنوع), the pre-eternity of species, and the like. These are issues built upon premises that were not proven in philosophy, yet the ignorant among the philosophizers presented them in the guise of proven issues. The materialists and their like, who at that time identified with philosophy, added other matters from their falsehoods, such as the doctrine of reincarnation (التناسخ) and the denial of the Resurrection (المعاد), especially the physical Resurrection (المعاد الجسماني). Through all of this, they attacked the apparent meanings of religion. Sometimes one of them would say: "Religion is a collection of traditional duties brought by the prophets to cultivate and perfect simple, naive minds; as for the philosopher who deals with the real sciences (العلوم الحقيقية), he has no need for them or what they brought." They were proficient in the methods of demonstration.

This prompted the jurists and theologians and drove them to confront them with rejection and to destroy them by any means available—through disputation, praying against them, disassociating from them, and declaring them disbelievers (تكفير)—until they broke their momentum, dispersed their gathering, and destroyed their books during the time of al-Mutawakkil. Philosophy almost became extinct after him until it was revived again by the Second Teacher, Abu Nasr al-Farabi (d. 339 AH), then after him by the Chief Master (الشيخ الرئيس), Abu Ali al-Husayn ibn Abd Allah ibn Sina (d. 428 AH), and then others among the luminaries of philosophy such as Abu Ali ibn Miskawayh, Ibn Rushd of Andalusia, and others. Since then, philosophy has continued to survive among a few practitioners, fluctuating between weakness and strength.

Although it was initially transferred to the Arabs, only a rare few among them became famous for it, such as al-Kindi and Ibn Rushd; it finally settled in Iran. Although the Muslim theologians opposed philosophy and rejected its people most vehemently, the majority of them accepted logic (المنطق), composing treatises and books on it because they found it consistent with the natural way of demonstration (الاستدلال الفطري).

However—as you have heard—they erred in its application. They applied the judgment of real definitions (الحدود الحقيقية) and their parts to conventional concepts (المفاهيم الاعتبارية), and they used demonstration (البرهان) in conventional propositions (القضايا الاعتبارية) where only dialectical syllogism (القياس الجدلي) is applicable. Thus, you see them discussing theological subjects such as beauty and ugliness, reward and punishment, the nullification of deeds (الحبط), and grace (الفضل) in terms of their genera (أجناسها), differentia (فصولها), and definitions (حدودها)—and where are these from a real definition? They use necessity (الضرورة) and impossibility (الامتناع) as evidence in issues of legal theory (الأصول) and theological issues from the branches of religion. This is an instance of employing realities (الحقائق) in conventional matters (الأمور الاعتبارية). They demonstrate regarding matters pertaining to the Necessary Being (الواجب تعالى) by saying: "It is necessary for Him to do such-and-such" and "It is ugly for Him to do such-and-such," thus judging realities by conventions and considering it a demonstration, while in reality, it is nothing but a poetic syllogism (القياس الشعري).

The excess in this chapter reached such a point that one of them said: "Allah, the Glorified, is too transcendent for convention (الاعتبار)—the reality of which is illusion (الوهم)—to creep into His judgment and action; therefore, everything He has brought into existence (تكوينًا) or legislated (تشريعًا) are real, factual matters." Another said: "Allah, the Glorified, is too powerful to decree a judgment and then be unable to establish a demonstration for it; thus, demonstration encompasses both the existential (التكوينيات) and the legislative (التشريعيات) realms together." These are among the sayings that are, by my life, among the tragedies of science and its people, and the necessity of recording and discussing them in scholarly writings is an even greater tragedy.

During this period, Sufism (التصوف) appeared among the Muslims. It had a root during the era of the Caliphs appearing in the garb of asceticism (الزهد). Then the matter became clear with the open display of the Sufis in the early Abbasid era with the appearance of men among them such as Abu Yazid, al-Junayd, al-Shibli, Ma'ruf, and others.

These people believe that the path to the reality of human perfection and the attainment of the realities of knowledge is to enter the Path (الطريقة) — *a method of spiritual discipline within the Shari'ah to attain the Truth* — which is a form of spiritual exercise (ارتياض) through the Shari'ah to reach the Truth (الحقيقة). Most of them, from both the elite and the commoners, attribute themselves to Ali (peace be upon him).

Since these people claimed matters of miracles (الكرامات) and spoke of things that contradict the apparent meanings of religion and the judgment of the intellect—claiming they have correct meanings that the understanding of the people of the apparent (أهل الظاهر) cannot reach—it was heavy upon the jurists and the general Muslims to hear them. They rejected them and met them with disassociation and charges of disbelief. They were sometimes imprisoned, lashed, killed, crucified, expelled, or exiled—all because of their lack of restraint and their indulgence in sayings they called "the secrets of the Shari'ah." If the matter were as they claimed, and these were the core of the Truth while the religious apparent meanings were like a shell over them, and it was necessary to manifest and proclaim them as they do, then the Legislator of the Shari'ah would have been more worthy of observing their state and announcing their matter as they announce it. And if they are not the truth, then what is after the truth except falsehood?

In the beginning, these people did not indicate their views on the Path except through speech. Then they added to that—after they had gained some place in the hearts—by composing books and treatises after the third century AH. Then they went further by explicitly stating their views on both the Truth and the Path after that, and what they composed in poetry and prose spread throughout the earth.

They continued to increase in number, preparation, influence in the hearts of the commoners, and prestige until they reached their absolute peak in the sixth and seventh centuries. Then they regressed in their course, their cause weakened, and the general public turned away from them.

The reason for their decline was, firstly, that when any vital matter that concerns the general public becomes the object of intense devotion and the hearts become infatuated with it, the souls of a group of ambitious people long to profit through its path. They then donned its garb and appeared in the form of its people and its elite, corrupting it, which was followed by the people's aversion to it.

Secondly, a group of their masters mentioned that the method of self-knowledge (معرفة النفس) is an innovated method (طريقة مبتدعة) that the Legislator of the Shari'ah did not mention in what He legislated, except that it is a pleasing path that Allah, the Glorified, accepted, just as He accepted the innovated monasticism among the Christians. The Exalted said: **وَرَهْبانِيَّةً ابْتَدَعُوها ما كَتَبْناها عَلَيْهِمْ إِلَّا ابْتِغاءَ رِضْوانِ اللهِ فَما رَعَوْها حَقَّ رِعايَتِها** — "And as for monasticism, they innovated it—We did not prescribe it for them—only seeking Allah’s pleasure; but they did not observe it with due observance." (57:27).

The majority of them accepted this, which allowed them to innovate rituals and etiquettes for the spiritual journey (السلوك) that were not known in the Shari'ah. Thus, they continued to innovate new traditions and abandon Shari'ah ones, until it led to the Shari'ah being on one side and the Path on the other. This naturally led to indulgence in prohibitions, the abandonment of obligatory duties from the religious rites, the lifting of religious obligations (رفع التكاليف), and the appearance of groups like the Qalandariyyah. Nothing remained of Sufism except begging and the use of opium and bhang, which they called "annihilation" (الفناء).

That which the Book and the Sunnah judge—and they both lead to the judgment of the intellect—is that the statement that beneath the apparent meanings of the Shari'ah are realities that constitute its inner depth (باطنها) is true. The statement that man has a path to attaining them is true. However, the path is only the proper use of the religious apparent meanings as they ought to be used, and nothing else. Far be it that there should be an inner reality to which the outer does not lead; the outer (الظاهر) is the title of the inner and its path. Far be it that there should be something else closer than what the Legislator of the religion indicated, which He overlooked, or was lenient about, or turned away from entirely for some reason, while He, the Mightiest of speakers, says: **وَنَزَّلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ تِبْياناً لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ** — "We have sent down the Book to you as an exposition of all things" (16:89). In short, these are three paths in the search for realities and their discovery: the religious apparent meanings, the path of intellectual inquiry, and the path of self-purification. Each was taken by a group of Muslims, despite the conflict and mutual rejection between the three groups. Their gathering in this is like the angles of a triangle: whenever you increase the magnitude of one, you decrease the other two, and vice versa.

Discourse on exegesis (التفسير) varied drastically according to the different orientations of the exegetes, meaning that scholarly inquiry was mostly imposed upon the Quran rather than the reverse, except in rare cases.

You have already learned that the Book confirms whatever is true from each of these paths. Far be it that there should be a true inner reality that its outer does not agree with, and far be it that there should be a truth, whether outer or inner, that true demonstration (البرهان الحق) rejects or contradicts.

Therefore, a group of scholars, with the scholarly capital they possessed and despite their different orientations, sought to reconcile the religious apparent meanings and gnosis (العرفان), such as Ibn al-Arabi, Abd al-Razzaq al-Kashani, Ibn Fahd, the Second Martyr (al-Shahid al-Thani), and al-Fayd al-Kashani.

Others sought to reconcile philosophy and gnosis, such as Abu Nasr al-Farabi, Shaykh al-Suhrawardi—the author of *al-Ishraq*—and Shaykh Sa'in al-Din Muhammad Turkah.

Others sought to reconcile the religious apparent meanings and philosophy, such as al-Qadi Sa'id and others.

Others sought to reconcile all of them, such as Ibn Sina in his exegeses and books, Sadr al-Muta'allihin al-Shirazi in his books and treatises, and several who came after him.

Despite all this, the deep-rooted disagreement remains as it was; the abundance of efforts to cut its root only increases its entrenchment, and the efforts to extinguish its fire only increase its blaze: "I found every amulet to be of no use." You do not see the people of any of these arts except that they accuse the others of ignorance, heresy (زندقة), or foolishness of opinion, while the commoners disassociate from them all.

All of this is because the Ummah failed on the first day to respond to the Book's call for social reflection: **وَاعْتَصِمُوا بِحَبْلِ اللَّهِ جَمِيعًا وَلَا تَفَرَّقُوا** — "Hold fast, all together, to Allah’s cord, and do not be divided." And the discourse has many branches.

O Allah, guide us to what pleases You regarding us, unite our word upon the truth, and grant us from Yourself a guardian, and grant us from Yourself a helper.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ قَدْ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولُنا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ كَثِيراً** — "O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you much..." (the verse): Ibn al-Daris, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Hakim—who authenticated it—narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Whoever disbelieves in stoning (الرجم) has disbelieved in the Quran from where he does not realize." The Exalted said: **يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ قَدْ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولُنا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ كَثِيراً مِمَّا كُنْتُمْ تُخْفُونَ مِنَ الْكِتابِ** — "O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you much of what you used to hide of the Book." He said: "Stoning was among what they hid."

I say: This is an allusion to what will come in the exegesis of His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ لا يَحْزُنْكَ** — "O Apostle! Do not let those who outpace each other in unfaith grieve you..." until the end of the verses (5:41), regarding the narration of the Jews concealing the ruling of stoning during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and his uncovering of that.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ عَلى فَتْرَةٍ مِنَ الرُّسُلِ** — "...clarifying for you after a gap between the apostles..." (the verse), he said: "He said: 'During an interruption of the apostles.'"

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Abu Hamzah Thabit ibn Dinar al-Thumali and Abu al-Rabi', who said: "We performed the pilgrimage with Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) in the year in which Hisham ibn Abd al-Malik performed the pilgrimage. With him was Nafi', the client of Umar ibn al-Khattab. He looked at Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) at the corner of the House (the Kaaba) while the people had gathered around him. Nafi' said: 'O Commander of the Faithful, who is this around whom the people are crowding?' He replied: 'This is the prophet of the people of Kufa; this is Muhammad ibn Ali.' He said: 'I bear witness that I shall go to him and ask him about matters that none can answer me except a prophet or the successor (وصي) of a prophet.' He said: 'Then go and ask him; perhaps you will embarrass him.'"

"So Nafi' came until he leaned against the people, then he looked over at Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) and said: 'O Muhammad ibn Ali, I have read the Torah, the Gospel, the Psalms, and the Criterion (الفرقان), and I have known their lawful and their prohibited. I have come to ask you about matters that none can answer except a prophet or the successor of a prophet.' Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) raised his head and said: 'Ask whatever occurs to you.' He said: 'Tell me, how many years were there between Jesus and Muhammad?' He said: 'Shall I tell you according to my word or according to your word?' He said: 'Tell me according to both words.' He said: 'As for my word, it is five hundred years; as for your word, it is six hundred years.'"

I say: Various reports have been narrated regarding the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول) for the verses, such as what al-Tabari narrated from Ikrimah: "The Jews asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about the ruling of stoning. He asked about the most learned among them, and they pointed to Ibn Suriya. He adjured him by Allah: 'Do they find the ruling of stoning in their book?' He replied: 'When it became frequent among us, we would lash a hundred and shave the heads.' So he judged them with stoning, and Allah revealed: **يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ** — 'O People of the Book...' until His saying: **صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ** — '...a straight path.'" And what he also narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Ibn Abi, Bahri ibn Amr, and Shas ibn Adi came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). He spoke to them and they spoke to him. He invited them to Allah and warned them of His vengeance. They said: 'What are you trying to frighten us with, O Muhammad? By Allah, we are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones'—like the saying of the Christians. So Allah revealed concerning them: **وَقالَتِ الْيَهُودُ وَالنَّصارى** — 'The Jews and the Christians say...'" (until the end of the verse).

And what he also narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah invited the Jews to Islam, encouraging them in it and warning them, but they refused him. Mu'adh ibn Jabal, Sa'd ibn Ubadah, and Uqbah ibn Wahb said to them: 'O assembly of Jews, be conscious of Allah! By Allah, you certainly know that he is the Messenger of Allah. You used to mention him to us before his mission and describe him to us by his attributes.' Rafi' ibn Huraymah and Wahb ibn Yahuda said: 'We never said this to you, and Allah has not sent down any book after Moses, nor has He sent any bearer of good news or warner after him.' So Allah revealed: **يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ قَدْ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولُنا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ عَلى فَتْرَةٍ** — 'O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you after a gap...'" (the verse). This was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from him and others, and other things were narrated as well.

The contents of these narrations, like most of what has been reported regarding theoretical occasions (أسباب نظرية), are merely applications of the cases to the contents of the verses and then judging them to be the occasions of revelation. Thus, they are theoretical occasions, while the verses are as if they were absolute in their revelation.
